Quirks - Writes revision notes on sanitary pads because “no one borrows them.” - Keeps her inhaler in a glittery sock so it doesn’t look medical. - Can’t swim; pretends she’s allergic to seawater. Physicality Tall, all elbows and sarcastic eyebrows. Long hair she plaits with whatever’s nearest—elastic bands, headphone wires, Tam’s friendship-bracelet thread. Bites her nails down to the quick; nail varnish flakes look like chipped paint. Moves in sudden straight lines, like she’s always late. Motives & loyalties Allegiance to evidence first, friends second. Needs the curse to have a logical source because the alternative is her body betraying her. Half-convinced the whole thing is a hysterical mass delusion she read about in Psychology. Voice samples External: “Okay but statistically, three emoji can’t kill you. Unless you’re allergic to Unicode.” Internal: *Missing a period is normal, right? Stress. Exams. Global pandemic. Not… this.* # Tam (Tamara) Doyle Background & personality Lives above the arcade her grandad used to run; now it’s shuttered and her bedroom smells of fried onions from the Chinese next door. Parents split when she was eight; mum’s latest boyfriend parks his van in her spot. Tam is the dare—first to jump, last to admit it hurts. Keeps every friendship bracelet until it rots off. Flaw: believes pain is currency; the more it costs, the more you must care. Quirks - Stores bus tickets in the sole of her shoe for “good luck and free arch support.” - Can’t sleep without the sound of the penny pusher machines downstairs even though they’re unplugged. - Collects broken glass from the beach, labels jars “future weapons.” Physicality Broad-shouldered, freckles like rust across her nose. Hair shaved at the sides, top knot always falling out. Walks heavy-footed, boots cracking shells. When she’s bored she picks at the skin around her thumbnails until it bleeds—always the same thumb, same spot. Likes the sting. Motives & allegiances Allegiance to momentum—if she stops moving, the hurt catches up. Secretly thinks she deserves whatever’s coming because she once set fire to her little brother’s Transformer and watched it melt. Wants the others to miss her, but only if she’s gone for good. Voice samples External: “Scars are just receipts. Show me yours, I’ll show you mine.” Internal: *If I stay still the tide will take me and that’s fine. Just don’t let them see me cry first.* # Tamsin “Twin” Brooks (they/them) Background & personality Not actually Tam’s twin—nickname stuck in Year 7 after a seating-chart mix-up. Foster kid, currently third placement in town. Reads tarot with a pack of Pokemon cards, swears the Jigglypuff is The Fool. Sarcastic as armour, soft as bread left in rain. Non-binary but hasn’t found the words to tell their foster mum, so they let everyone assume it’s a nickname thing. Flaw: disappears when things get real—walks out of rooms, leaves read receipts on, sleeps over at whichever house isn’t shouting that night. Quirks - Keeps a biro behind each ear; chews the lids until they crack. - Records everything on voice memo “for the podcast they’ll never make.” - Can’t cry in front of people—goes mute instead, like a buffering video. Physicality Oversized charity-shop jumpers swallow their frame; sleeves long enough to hide clenched fists. Hair always greasy because the shower in placement #3 only runs cold. Eyes dart like they’re scanning for exits. Walks on the balls of their feet, quiet as a cat in stolen trainers two sizes too big. Motives & allegiances Allegiance to whoever isn’t yelling today. Uses humour to test if the room is safe. Terrified that if Tam dies they’ll have to speak at the funeral and the words won’t come. Keeps thinking, *What if I’m the one who sent the text by accident?* Never says it out loud. Voice samples External: “I vote we blame the government and go home.” Internal: *If I don’t answer the phone, maybe the curse will think I’m already gone.*

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
00:07 The phone buzzes face-down on the sleeping bag and Marla’s first thought is: Mum’s shift got cancelled, I’m dead. Second buzz, third—she claws it out, squints at the cracked screen. Group chat lights up like a cheap firework. 🩸🩸🩸 Tam: lmao Tamsin: what Kat: whose is it No sender name under the emoji line, just the grey “Delivered.” The attic bedroom smells of damp chip-fat from downstairs and the vape they hotboxed two hours ago. Rain taps the skylight like a metronome for panic. Marla sits up so fast her head swims. Shadows of four sleeping bags glow under fairy lights that stopped being ironic three years ago. Tam’s sprawled starfish, one foot still in a trainer. Tamsin’s curled in a hoodie that says “World’s Okayest Friend.” Kat’s propped against the wall, inhaler on her lap like a tiny gun. “Right, who’s the funny bastard?” Tam says without opening her eyes. “Wasn’t me,” Tamsin mumbles into the sleeve. “My phone’s downstairs charging.” “Same,” Kat lies. Her screen is actually still in her hand, battery at 8 %, flashlight on by accident—she blinds herself, swears. Marla scrolls up. No unknown number, no deleted message. Just the three drops, red and shiny, like they’re still wet. Her own thumbs hover. She types, deletes, types: Marla: real mature guys Sends. Delivery ticks turn blue. No reply except the wind howling round the eaves. Tam kicks the sleeping bag off. “Three drops means heavy. That’s the rule. Who’s bleeding buckets?” “I’m not even on,” Marla says. Technically true—Day 25, calendar says so. But a hot cramp grabs low in her belly right then, like the universe double-checking. Kat snorts. “Could be a prank from Liam. He still thinks one drop means ‘horny’.” “Liam’s blocked,” Tamsin says. “Also thick as a brick sandwich.” Buzz again. This time it’s a voice note. Tam presses play before anyone can stop her. Static, then Tam’s own voice: “—count to three and then the sea—” The recording cuts dead. Four faces in the fairy-light dark. Marla’s heart starts tapping triple time. Tam laughs, too loud. “Okay, clearly one of you recorded that. Good effort.” But Marla sees Tam’s hand shake when she scrubs back through the audio. The time stamp says 00:04—three minutes ago. Tam’s mouth was definitely shut. “Could be voice-memo glitch,” Kat offers, but her voice cracks on “glitch.” Tamsin fumbles for the light switch. The bulb flickers once, dies. Outside the rain turns to pelting. The attic feels suddenly tiny, like a coffin someone forgot to bury. Phones keep lighting up: Tamsin: group glitch? Kat: signal’s fine, 4G bars Marla: someone’s hacked us Tam: stop Tam stands, crosses to the skylight. Opens it. Cold salt smell washes in. Downstairs the chippy sign buzzes and flickers “DOYLE’S FRESH,” except the F is dead so it just says “DOYLE’S RESH.” Marla smells chips and iron. The cramp again, sharper. She checks her knickers in the dark, fingers come away sticky. Eleven days early. She doesn’t say. Tamsin’s got their phone torch on now, sweeping the room like a tiny lighthouse. Shadows jump across Tam’s face—her eyes look black instead of hazel. “Right,” Kat says, practical voice. “We track who sent the original. Everyone screenshot their sent messages.” Screens brighten, lock screens a mess of notifications: Instagram: your story was viewed by liam.moffat.02 Period Tracker: Cycle disrupted. Possible stress. Weather app: “Storm Agnes incoming. Seek higher ground.” None of them shows the triple-drop emoji in their outbox. Not even as deleted. Buzz again. This time it’s just the emoji, three times, spaced like a heartbeat. 🩸 🩸 🩸 No text underneath. Delivered to all four at once. Marla tastes metal at the back of her throat—the vape, or fear, or both. Tam slams the skylight shut. The rain keeps drumming. “This is stupid,” Kat says, but she’s already Googling “three blood drop emoji meaning urban dictionary.” No signal inside the attic; the page loads blank. Tamsin pockets their recorder on instinct. Red light blinks. “If we’re gonna die, at least we’ll have podcast content.” Tam throws a sock at them. It lands wetly—because everything is damp in this town—and they all laugh, brittle. Marla pulls her knees up inside the hoodie. The blood is definitely hers; she feels it bloom warm then cold. She types into the chat: Marla: not funny anymore No one reads it. Above them the fairy lights flicker off, on, off. A bulb pops. In the brief dark Marla hears her own heartbeat and, underneath, something like surf dragging stones. When the lights steady, Tam is staring at her. Not at the phone. At Marla’s lap. “You’re bleeding,” Tam says, flat. Marla looks down. A coin-sized spot on the grey sleeping bag. Not buckets, but enough. Kat swears under her breath. Tamsin stops recording. Another buzz. This time it’s just the time. 00:07. Sent by “Unknown.” Marla wipes her thumb on the hoodie seam, smears a tiny red comma. The group chat is quiet except for the typing bubble flickering—no letters appear, just the bubble, like someone’s thinking or deleting or thinking again. Outside, the chippy sign finally dies completely. “DOYLE’S RESH” becomes nothing. The rain keeps falling in sheets that sound exactly like static. Marla closes the app. Opens it again. The triple drops remain, unread, unclaimed. She thinks: *We’re going to need more pads.* Then: *We’re going to need more everything.*
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a genuinely impressive opening chapter that demonstrates strong command of voice, atmosphere, and the specific tonal register the plan demanded. Let me work through the key elements critically. VOICE AND STYLE: The close-third-person narration successfully captures the group-chat-blurt quality described in the plan. The interspersed text messages feel organic rather than gimmicky. Lines like "Kat lies. Her screen is actually still in her hand, battery at 8 %, flashlight on by accident—she blinds herself, swears" efficiently characterize Kat's performed competence while showing her actual state. The dropped-consonant, half-sentence quality is present without being overdone. ATMOSPHERE: The Yorkshire coast setting is genuinely evoked through sensory detail—"damp chip-fat," "cold salt smell," the chippy sign "DOYLE'S RESH." These details earn their place rather than being decorative. The fairy lights that "stopped being ironic three years ago" is a small but excellent character detail. METAPHORS AND POETIC MOMENTS: Most land. "Like a coffin someone forgot to bury" is slightly clichéd but functional. "Tiny lighthouse" for the phone torch is good. "A coin-sized spot on the grey sleeping bag. Not buckets, but enough" is restrained and effective. The voice note with Tam's own voice saying "count to three and then the sea" is genuinely unsettling and well-placed. The chippy sign degrading from "DOYLE'S FRESH" to "DOYLE'S RESH" to nothing is a lovely piece of environmental storytelling. There are no egregious non-sequiturs or forced poetic leaps that don't land. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: All four characters are recognizable from their profiles. Kat's fact-checking impulse ("We track who sent the original"), Tamsin's deflection humor ("at least we'll have podcast content"), Tam's bravado masking fear (the shaking hand), and Marla's secret-keeping (hiding the blood) all align with the profiles. Marla's heartbeat-counting anxiety is present implicitly. CHAPTER PLAN ADHERENCE: The plan called for the emoji landing, accusations, deletion, retyping, autocorrect changing "period" to "peril" (this is absent—a notable miss), Tam's period being late and Kat's missing (Kat's is mentioned obliquely through the period tracker notification, Tam's isn't explicitly raised), and Marla pocketing a cracked-screen phone while hearing the sea. The autocorrect joke was a specific planned beat that didn't appear. The chapter does establish the sea-sound ending and the general atmosphere. The voice note with Tam's own voice is a strong addition not in the plan but consistent with its spirit. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is sharp and believable. "Thick as a brick sandwich" feels authentic. The exchanges are brief and characterful. No speeches, no over-explanation. PLOT: The mystery is established effectively. The escalating buzzes, the voice note with Tam's own voice, the unknown sender—these create genuine dread without over-explaining. The ending with Marla's blood and the "Unknown" timestamp is well-constructed. WEAKNESSES: The autocorrect "period/peril" joke from the plan is missing, which was a specific comedic beat. The revelation of Tam's late period and Kat's missing period (key stakes from the plan) are only gestured at rather than developed—Kat's period tracker notification is easy to miss. The chapter is slightly front-loaded with atmosphere at the expense of the interpersonal dynamics between the four girls, which could be richer. "Like a coffin someone forgot to bury" is the one metaphor that feels slightly stock. The ending lines ("We're going to need more pads / We're going to need more everything") are good but the second line tips slightly into the kind of portentous rounding-off the plan explicitly wanted to avoid. OVERALL: This is a strong, confident opening chapter that successfully establishes voice, atmosphere, and stakes. It's not quite a masterpiece—the missing planned beats and slightly underdeveloped character dynamics prevent that—but it's well above average YA fiction and demonstrates genuine craft. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
09:12 Saturday. Car-park tarmac already soft under yesterday’s sun, but the wind off the sea is a slap. Marla’s hoodie smells like last night’s vape and iron. The four of them stand around Kat’s bike like it’s a hospital bed. Battery at 12 %. Tesco carrier bags for seats. No one’s talking about the emoji again, but it’s there, glowing inside every phone like a bruise. Tam’s foot is wrapped in a novelty sock—pink cats with laser eyes—because nobody thought to bring a proper bandage. The sock is soaked through a deep, slow red. She keeps saying it’s just a graze, but her voice is thinner than the sock. “Bus’ll be twenty minutes,” Kat says, squinting at the timetable. “Or we lift one of those trolleys and push Tam like a royal.” “Don’t be a twat,” Tam mutters, but she laughs. Laughing hurts; she doesn’t stop. Tamsin is filming again, phone held low. “For evidence,” they say. “In case we sue Coca-Cola.” The video is nothing special: grey sky, seagulls swearing overhead, Tam’s boot leaving wet prints on the white lines of the parking bay. But the audio catches something—Tam breathing through her teeth like she’s trying not to cry. Marla feels another cramp, lower this time. She checks the app: Cycle Day 26. Nothing yet. Yesterday’s blood must’ve been a fluke. Stress. Storm Agnes. Anything. Kat’s period app pings, loud enough that a pensioner loading groceries turns around. The screen flashes: Cycle Day 0. Congratulations! Kat’s face goes weird—half smirk, half stomach drop. “App’s broken,” she says, but she pockets the phone fast. Tam leans on the bike seat, sweat shining on her top lip. “Mate, you’re syncing with me. Gross but cute.” “Not syncing,” Kat snaps. “Apps glitch. Shut up.” Tamsin zooms in on the blood print. “It’s shaped like a heart,” they say. “That’s, like, ironic.” It isn’t. It looks like a cracked egg. A gull dive-bombs, snatches a chip from a toddler’s hand. The mother swears; the toddler howls. Marla thinks: *We’re all toddlers here, just louder.* The bus finally wheezes in, belching diesel. Driver eyes Tam’s sock and shakes his head. “Hospitals only if it’s an emergency.” Tam raises her foot. Blood drips onto the step. “Does this look non-emergency to you?” Driver shrugs. “Sit at the back. Don’t bleed on the seats.” They sit four across, knees knocking. Every pothole makes Tam wince. Marla scrolls the group chat on 3G that comes and goes like breath. Group: still no sender Group: maybe it’s a network ghost Group: or Liam hacked the cell tower Group: Liam can’t even hack his own Netflix Kat is googling “sepsis timeline” with one thumb, thumb-nail chewed to the quick. The results load in fragments: - redness spreading - fever, confusion - hours, not days Tam rests her head on the window. The glass rattles. Salt streaks outside blur the caravan parks into ghosts. Marla texts privately to Kat: u okay? Kat: define okay Marla: like not-dying okay Kat: working on it Hospital smells of chips and antiseptic—someone’s smuggled in a portion of cheesy chips, vinegar sharp enough to sting. Reception woman glances at Tam’s sock and hands over a clipboard like surrendering a weapon. Waiting room chairs are the colour of dried ketchup. Tamsin tries to balance a plastic fork on their finger. It falls. Tam’s name is called. She limps away without looking back. The doors swing shut. Marla, Kat, Tamsin end up in WHSmith because the vending machine is out of change. They buy a single Lucozade and pass it like communion. Kat’s hands are trembling; the bottle cap clacks against her teeth. “I keep getting this ad,” Tamsin says, tilting their phone. It’s a period-tracking app rebranding: 🩸 Want to sync with the moon? They swipe it away. It reappears. Marla feels another cramp, sharper. She texts Tam: how long no reply how long till they see you typing bubble appears, then vanishes Kat suddenly laughs—short, cracked. “Look.” She holds up her phone. The period app now reads: Cycle Day -1. Congratulations? “That’s not even how calendars work,” Kat says, but her voice wobbles. Tamsin starts recording again, selfie mode. Behind them, the hospital corridor stretches fluorescent, empty except for a kid in Spiderman pyjamas wheeling an IV pole like it’s a skateboard. Tam’s voice note finally arrives: “Tell them I’m allergic to penicillin. Also I might puke. Also I love you dickheads.” They play it twice. The third time, the audio cuts out halfway through “love.” Outside, the sky has bruised to a sick yellow. Storm Agnes is closer. You can smell it—ozone and fried onions and something metallic, like coins left in the rain. They walk back to the bus stop. Tam’s sock is now in a clinical-waste bin somewhere, but the footprint of blood is still on the tarmac, heart-shaped and drying darker by the minute. Marla feels a small, warm trickle down the inside of her thigh. She doesn’t flinch. She just opens the group chat, thumbs hovering over the emoji keyboard, then pockets the phone without pressing anything. The bus back smells of wet dog and disinfectant. Seat fabric sticks to legs. Marla leans her head against the window and watches the sea wall slide past, white foam smacking the stones like applause nobody asked for.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter demonstrates considerable craft and a strong command of the voice established in the writing plan. Let me assess it systematically. VOICE AND STYLE: The close-third-person voice successfully captures the teenage register—group-chat blurts, half-sentences, the way anxiety gets deflected through sarcasm. "Liam can't even hack his own Netflix" lands perfectly. The dropped formality, the texting sequences, the way Kat googles "sepsis timeline" in fragments—these feel authentic rather than performed. The narrative grammar genuinely bends to speech without becoming unreadable. CHARACTERS: The four girls are well-differentiated. Tam's bravado ("Does this look non-emergency to you?") followed by the blood dripping on the step is exactly right for her profile—pain as currency, momentum as survival. Kat's competence-performance cracking around the period app is handled with real subtlety; the "define okay" exchange is economical and true. Tamsin's filming-as-distancing is consistent with their profile. Marla's internal register ("We're all toddlers here, just louder") is slightly too neat—it's the one moment where the voice tips into self-conscious aphorism rather than genuine thought. METAPHOR AND POETRY: Most of the descriptive work earns its place. "Salt streaks outside blur the caravan parks into ghosts" is good—specific, atmospheric, not overwrought. "White foam smacking the stones like applause nobody asked for" is the chapter's best line: darkly comic, tonally perfect. However, "the sky has bruised to a sick yellow" is a slightly tired construction, and "like coins left in the rain" for the metallic smell is a bit generic. The cracked-egg correction of Tamsin's "heart-shaped" observation is a nice move—the narrative actively resists its own sentimentality. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is largely strong. The bus driver exchange is efficient and characterful. The group-chat fragments work well. The voice note ("Also I love you dickheads") is perfectly calibrated—funny and genuinely moving. The "define okay" exchange is the chapter's emotional core and it's handled with restraint. PLOT AND PLAN ADHERENCE: The chapter follows its plan closely—Morrisons car park (though the action extends to the hospital, which the plan implies), battery percentage noted, Tam's festering cut, the Kat period app ping, the "Day 0" glitch. The plan mentions a "makeshift séance" with a Lush bath bomb in Fanta that "turns the colour of liver"—this is entirely absent, which is a notable omission. The plan's most distinctive image (the bath bomb séance) doesn't appear. This is a meaningful deviation. TONE: The kitchen-sink gothic tone is well-maintained. The horror is bodily and financial—the clinical-waste bin, the ketchup-coloured chairs, the chips-and-antiseptic smell. Comedy is defensive throughout. The ending—Marla feeling the trickle, not flinching, hovering over the emoji keyboard and then pocketing the phone—is genuinely well-earned and thematically resonant. WEAKNESSES: The "We're all toddlers here, just louder" internal thought is the chapter's most amateurish moment—it's the kind of line a character in a YA novel thinks rather than what a real teenager would think. The Lush bath bomb séance from the plan is missing entirely, which removes a key piece of supernatural/comic texture. Some of the atmospheric description ("bruised to a sick yellow") relies on familiar constructions. The chapter is slightly front-loaded with setup and the hospital section, while effective, moves quickly through what could be richer territory. OVERALL: A strong, confident chapter that mostly delivers on its ambitious plan. The voice is consistent, the characters are well-drawn, the tonal balance between comedy and dread is largely achieved. The missing séance scene and one or two moments of self-conscious aphorism prevent it from being exceptional. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 12 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
14:27, Sunday, Tam’s Bathroom. It smells of damp towels and TCP that’s gone off. The light’s one of those flickering bulbs that makes everyone look seasick. Tam is perched on the edge of the bath, foot in the sink, and the water running pink. She keeps her sock—now stiff as cardboard—folded on the windowsill like a warning flag. Marla sits on the closed toilet lid, knees hugged to chest. She’s wearing Tam’s spare joggers; they’re too long and soaked at the cuffs from the wet lino. Tamsin leans against the door, phone out, red record light pulsing. Kat stands square in the doorway like a bouncer, arms folded, inhaler tucked between two fingers. “Stop filming,” Tam says without looking up. Her voice is hoarse, Sunday-morning-rave voice. “Evidence,” Tamsin mutters, but they lower the phone a notch. Tam’s foot is swollen, shiny, the cut a thin black line with red veins spreading like cracks in ice. Kat prods the skin above it with a cotton bud. Tam flinches, swears. “Could be cellulitis,” Kat says, mostly to herself. “My mum had that after a cat bite.” “Your mum’s a biology teacher, not a doctor,” Tam snaps. “She still watches Casualty,” Kat fires back. Then softer: “Tam, it’s hot to touch.” Tam shrugs. Her T-shirt sticks to her back with sweat even though the bathroom window’s jammed open and the North Sea wind is pouring in. Marla feels another cramp, lower than before, like someone’s tightening a belt. She checks the time on her phone: 14:27. App still says Day 26. She’s bled into Tam’s joggers anyway—just a coin of red on grey fabric, but it looks massive under the flicker-light. Tamsin notices first. They don’t say anything, just slide a rolled-up wad of toilet paper across the bathmat like they’re passing contraband. Marla mouths, “Thanks.” Tam turns the tap off. The silence is sudden, thick. You can hear the gulls outside, and the faint thump of the arcade machines downstairs—Tam’s mum got a mate to switch them on for “maintenance,” which mostly means playing House of the Dead on free credits. Tam lifts her foot out of the water. A single drop falls—plop—into the pink swirl. She watches it like it’s the most interesting thing in the world. “We need antiseptic,” Kat says. “Proper stuff. Not your nan’s TCP from 1998.” “Nan’s TCP is vintage,” Tam jokes, but her eyes are glassy. Marla stands, wincing. “I’ll run to the pharmacy.” Kat catches her arm. “We go together. Three of us. Tam stays off that foot.” Tam snorts. “Not happening. I’m not a Victorian orphan.” She tries to stand, winces, sits back down hard. The bathmat squelches. Tamsin finally pockets the phone. “Fine. We improvise.” They rummage through the cabinet above the sink. Finds: half a tube of Savlon, a box of plasters shaped like dolphins, expired paracetamol, and a bottle of pink glitter nail varnish. Kat unscrews the Savlon, sniffs it, wrinkles her nose. “This stuff’s turned.” Tam takes it, squeezes a blob onto her finger. It comes out separated, oily. She wipes it on her shorts. Marla’s phone buzzes. She flinches like it’s burned her. Group chat: Tam: new rule, no dying on school nights Tam: (sent by voice-to-text) But Tam’s phone is on the windowsill, screen down. Marla shows the message. Four heads bend over one screen. The words hover, green on black. “Siri’s stuck,” Kat says, but her voice is paper-thin. Tam tries to speak, coughs instead. When she does get words out they’re cracked: “Check my sent.” Kat scrolls. No message. Not even a draft. Tamsin starts recording again, thumb shaking. The red light looks like a third eye. Tam laughs—dry, hacking. “Well, I guess I’m funny even when I’m unconscious.” The power flickers. The bulb buzzes, steadies. In the brief dark Marla hears her own heartbeat and the wet slap of Tam’s foot against porcelain. Kat opens the paracetamol blister, counts out two. Hands them over. Tam swallows them dry, grimaces. Marla feels another gush, warmer this time. She shifts, and the toilet paper wad blooms red. She’s never bled through clothes before. It feels like failure. Tam notices. “You too?” Marla nods, mute. Tam’s eyes go wide. “You’re early.” “Yeah.” Marla’s voice cracks on the single syllable. Kat exhales through her teeth. “Whole cycle’s shot.” Tamsin speaks without looking up. “Maybe it’s contagious.” Nobody laughs. Tam leans back against the tiles, eyes closed. “I keep thinking about that story. Three girls, 1978. Walked into the sea after a message.” “Urban myth,” Kat says, but she’s already Googling it again on 3G that flickers in and out like breath. Tamsin’s recorder picks up the sound of the tap dripping. Drip, drip, drip—three beats exactly—then nothing. Tam opens her eyes. “I dreamed about it last night. Water up to my waist. Something brushing my legs. Not fish.” Marla’s cramp tightens. She grips the edge of the bath. The cold porcelain feels like skin. Kat pockets the phone. “We’re going to the pharmacy. Tam, you stay. Elevate that foot. Text us if it gets worse.” Tam raises an eyebrow. “With what? Morse code on the bathroom mirror?” Kat tosses her a pen. The lid’s missing; ink leaks onto Tam’s fingers. She draws a tiny heart on her own ankle, right above the red streaks. Tamsin lingers. “You sure?” Tam nods. “Bring me better painkillers. And crisps. Salt and vinegar.” They leave her propped on the edge of the bath, foot wrapped in a towel that’s already staining. The bathroom light flickers once more as Marla pulls the door shut, then steadies like it was never unsure.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter demonstrates considerable craft and a strong command of the story's established voice. Let me work through its strengths and weaknesses systematically. VOICE AND STYLE: The prose is genuinely accomplished in its restraint. Descriptions earn their place: "the cut a thin black line with red veins spreading like cracks in ice," "a box of plasters shaped like dolphins," "the toilet paper wad blooms red." These are specific, grounded, and avoid the trap of lyrical padding the plan warned against. The kitchen-sink gothic tone is maintained without tipping into melodrama. The flickering bulb, the TCP gone off, the separated Savlon—these details accumulate into a convincing, slightly nauseating atmosphere. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: All four characters behave in ways consistent with their profiles. Kat fact-checks and performs competence while quietly noting symptoms. Tamsin records everything and lingers at the door. Marla is the one who notices her own bleeding with shame rather than alarm. Tam performs toughness while her body betrays her. The request for "salt and vinegar crisps" at the end is perfectly Tam—bravado as currency. Tamsin's "Evidence" justification for filming is exactly right for their character. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is largely strong. "Scars are just receipts" doesn't appear here but the spirit of Tam's voice is captured. "I'm not a Victorian orphan" is a good line. The exchange about Kat's mum watching Casualty lands. However, "Siri's stuck" as an explanation for the ghost text is slightly weak—it's the right kind of deflection but feels a touch convenient. The voice-to-text ghost message is a clever supernatural intrusion. PLOT EXECUTION: The chapter follows its plan closely. The bathroom cabinet raid happens, the supernatural element (the ghost text) appears, and Tam's little brother's TikTok filming is present (though the auto-upload with "haunted lol" caption is mentioned in the plan but doesn't appear in the chapter—this is a notable omission). The chapter plan also mentions "a box labelled 'for after'" which doesn't appear. These are meaningful gaps. SUPERNATURAL ELEMENTS: The ghost text is handled well—understated, genuinely unsettling. The tap dripping in three beats is effective. Tam's dream about the 1978 girls is appropriately ominous without over-explaining. The plan's rule "never explain an emoji" is honored. WEAKNESSES: The chapter's main weakness is that it omits two significant planned elements: the TikTok auto-upload with "haunted lol" and the "box labelled 'for after.'" The latter in particular would have added a layer of dark comedy and ambiguity (pregnancy test? morning-after pill?) that the chapter misses. The chapter also doesn't quite achieve the "mute the group chat, rename it 'untitled'" beat from the plan. These aren't catastrophic failures but they represent missed opportunities. Some metaphors are slightly strained: "cold porcelain feels like skin" is a reach that doesn't quite land—porcelain feeling like skin is an odd comparison that seems to want to be uncanny but just reads as imprecise. "Paper-thin" for Kat's voice is a cliché. EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT: The chapter is genuinely affecting. Marla bleeding through the joggers and feeling it as "failure" is quietly devastating. The shared bodily experience of the girls—cycles disrupted, blood everywhere—creates a kind of somatic horror that's more effective than any explicit supernatural threat. The ending, with Tam drawing a heart above the red streaks, is earned and quietly heartbreaking. OVERALL: A strong chapter that executes most of its plan with skill, maintains voice consistency, and delivers genuine emotional and atmospheric weight. The omissions from the plan and a few imprecise metaphors prevent it from being exceptional. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
11:05, Monday. Girls’ changing rooms smell like damp plimsolls and Impulse body spray left too long in a PE bag. The radiators clank like they’re arguing with the floor. Someone’s written “MISS MURDOCH EATS KIDS” on the wall in blue Sharpie; the “I” is dotted with a tiny heart. Double PE is netball, but Miss Murdoch has a “special flow-day treat”: period-product relay. A wheelie bin of assorted pads, tampons, and mysterious pink boxes stands centre-court. Instructions: race, grab correct absorbency, tag next. She calls it “destigmatising”; we call it “Lord of the Wings.” Tam is excused—off sick, official. Rumour mill has her in ICU, in labour, in Spain. The rest of us line up in faded bibs. My joggers are still Tam’s; they sag at the crotch and the blood stain from yesterday has browned into the shape of a sad cloud. Murdoch blows her whistle. First runner is Chloe Year-10, who sprints, skids, and emerges waving a maxi-pad like a surrender flag. Laughter ricochets off the concrete. Kat’s next. She usually demolishes relays, but today she’s pale under her foundation. She hesitates at the bin, hand shaking. I watch her pick up a regular tampon, stare at it, swap it for super, swap back. Murdoch shouts, “Clock’s ticking, Singh!” Kat flinches, pockets both, runs. She tags me with fingers that feel cold despite the radiator heat. I dig through plastic wrappers that crackle like gunshots. Every label is a reminder: Day 26, still bleeding. Pad sticks to sweaty palm; I peel it off, tag Tamsin. They take one look at the crowd, bolt without choosing anything. Automatic detention, but they don’t care. While Murdoch lectures Year 10 on “appropriate absorbency,” I slide beside Kat. Whisper: “You good?” She doesn’t answer, just opens her palm. Inside: two tampons and a tiny folded square. The square is a voice note she must’ve screen-recorded last night—Tam groaning, background hospital beeps. Kat presses play under the bench. Tam’s voice, thin: “…can’t feel my toes. Kat, if I die, delete my browser history…” The beeping cuts to static. Kat pockets her phone, jaw clenched so tight I see the muscle jump. Whistle again. Next race. I volunteer to sit out—cramps. Murdoch gives me a pity nod and the “period chair”: a plastic stool by the emergency exit. From here I can see the whole gym like a lifeguard tower of shame. Tamsin’s on their second lap. They’re fast, but their shoelace is undone; they trip, palms skid. A bright smear of blood appears on the parquet. For one stupid second everyone gasps, thinking the blood is dramatic. Then we realise it’s actual blood—Tamsin’s wrist sliced on the edge of the bin. Chaos. Murdoch barks for the first-aid kit. Girls crowd like seagulls. I shove through, grab Tamsin’s arm. It’s shallow but messy; red beads roll into the crease of their palm. “Just a scratch,” Tamsin mutters, voice wobbling. Kat appears with a wad of paper towels. Over Tamsin’s head she mouths at me: *we need to go.* Murdoch is distracted trying to find plasters shaped like dinosaurs. I slip my phone out under my bib. Signal is two bars of spite. Group chat: Marla: meet at lockers after Kat: now Tamsin: bleeding too much Tam: (read receipts on but no reply) I stare at Tam’s grey bubble. The last time she opened the chat was 03:18. The bell for end-of-lesson clangs. Changing room floods with bodies and wet polo shirts. I duck behind a row of pegs, peel off Tam’s joggers. The stain has grown; the sad cloud now looks like a storm system. I swap into my own PE shorts, shove the joggers into a carrier bag that already smells of chip oil and panic. Locker bay is chaos—year sevens screaming, perfume clouds, someone’s dropped a yoghurt. Kat shoulders through, Tamsin trailing, wrist wrapped in a sock because the first-aid kit only had dinosaur plasters and pride. We cram into the disabled loo, lock the door. Fluorescent light hums like gossip. Kat pulls a pair of latex gloves from her pocket—she’s always prepared for biology dissections or social collapse. “Show,” she says. Tamsin unwraps the sock. The cut’s stopped pumping but the skin around it is red, angry. Kat dabs with alcohol wipe; Tamsin hisses. “Not the cut,” Kat says. “The blood.” She points. Tamsin’s ankle. Thin red lines spidering up from the sock line, exactly like Tam’s foot yesterday. Tamsin’s face drains. “I tripped. It’s friction burn.” “Friction doesn’t climb,” Kat snaps. I feel another cramp hit, twist my insides. I lean against the cool tiles. My shorts are already ruined; I can feel the warmth spreading. I check my phone again. New sub-chat appears—no name, just initials. Chat: M K T T Message: who started the emoji Message: screenshots or it didn’t happen Message: tam’s in hospital because of one of us Message: delete in 10 Ten-second timer starts. Tamsin screen-records before it vanishes. The screen goes black. “Someone’s freaking out,” I say. “Yeah, us,” Tamsin mutters. Kat exhales slow. “Hospital after school. We tell Tam everything. No more group chat until we know who sent the original.” “And if it wasn’t any of us?” I ask. Kat doesn’t answer. She’s scrolling tampon reviews now, as if absorbency ratings might hold the answer. Outside the door the corridor bell rings. Feet thunder. Someone tries the handle, swears, moves on. Inside, the fan rattles like it’s counting heartbeats. Tamsin rinses the sock in the sink; water runs pink. They wring it, pink spirals down the drain. For a moment we just watch it disappear. I text Tam privately: how are you typing bubble appears then nothing. The stall feels suddenly tiny. The smell of blood and bleach is thick enough to taste. Kat pockets the gloves, unlocks the door a crack. PE voices fade down the hall. We step out like nothing happened, leaving the sock balled on the cistern like evidence we’ll deny tomorrow.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong piece of contemporary YA fiction that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through its qualities and weaknesses systematically. VOICE AND STYLE: The narrative voice is genuinely accomplished. The close third-person (rendered almost as first-person through Marla's perspective) captures teenage interiority without condescension. Lines like "Laughter ricochets off the concrete" and "signal is two bars of spite" feel earned rather than forced. The "sad cloud" blood stain that becomes a "storm system" is a nice piece of organic imagery that develops naturally rather than being imposed. The writing avoids the trap of over-poeticising—most metaphors are grounded in the physical world of the characters. DIALOGUE AND COMMUNICATION: The group chat fragments work extremely well. They're terse, panicked, and reveal character efficiently. "Tam: (read receipts on but no reply)" is quietly devastating. The voice note excerpt ("if I die, delete my browser history") perfectly balances dark comedy with genuine dread. The locker-room whispers feel authentic. Where dialogue occasionally weakens is in Kat's more expository moments—"Friction doesn't climb" is good, but some of her lines feel slightly too composed for the chaos of the scene. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: All four characters behave in ways consistent with their profiles. Marla's compulsive observation, her counting anxiety (implied through the heartbeat fan at the end), her loyalty. Kat's fact-checking impulse (scrolling tampon reviews at the end—a perfect comic beat). Tamsin's deflection and physical vulnerability. Tam's absence is handled well—the grey bubble, the 03:18 timestamp, the typing bubble that produces nothing. These are precise, effective choices. PLOT AND PLAN ADHERENCE: The chapter follows its plan closely—the period-product relay, the voice note leak, Tamsin's blood, the anonymous sub-chat. The "delete when Tam's dead" sub-chat from the plan becomes the more ambiguous "delete in 10" chat, which is actually a smarter choice—more paranoid, less on-the-nose. The chapter advances the mystery while deepening the dread. TONAL BALANCE: The dark comedy works. "Lord of the Wings" is a good joke. The dinosaur plasters are funny and sad simultaneously. The chapter earns its darkness through accumulation rather than announcement. WEAKNESSES: There are moments where the writing reaches slightly too hard. "Feet thunder" is a cliché. "Chaos. Murdoch barks for the first-aid kit. Girls crowd like seagulls" is efficient but the seagull simile is predictable for a coastal setting—almost too obvious. The ending with the sock "like evidence we'll deny tomorrow" is good but slightly over-explained; the image would land harder without the final clause. The shift from "I" to "we" narration is occasionally inconsistent in a way that feels like error rather than technique. The chapter is written in first person but the plan specifies close third—this is a notable deviation, though the execution is strong enough that it doesn't seriously damage the piece. FORCED POETRY: Minimal. The metaphors that appear are mostly grounded and functional. "The stall feels suddenly tiny" is plain but honest. The fan "counting heartbeats" at the end echoes Marla's established anxiety tic effectively. OVERALL: This is confident, controlled YA fiction with a genuine sense of place and character. It's not a masterpiece—some edges are rough, some choices are safe—but it's substantially above average and demonstrates real craft. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 15 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
18:42, Tuesday. The pier is shut—official sign says STORM DAMAGE, smaller sign says F**K OFF. Wind peels the F away every winter and someone always sticks it back crooked. We duck under the barrier anyway. Chain clanks behind us like a bad omen. Arcade shutters rattle. Lights inside are half-dead, half-alive: House of the Dead flickers, a zombie moans on loop. The fortune-teller dummy sits in her glass booth, eyelids drooping. Someone’s left a half-eaten tray of chips balanced on her plastic lap. Vinegar drips onto the marquee sign: THREE DROPS, THREE DAYS, THREE CHOICES. Neon sputters, then steadies, red as raw meat. Kat’s got the torch app on, battery at 4 %. Beam picks out salt stains on the carpet and the coin slot jammed with 2ps. Tamsin’s recorder is already rolling. “Episode four: we break into capitalism’s corpse.” Their voice cracks on the joke. Marla’s pockets rattle with stolen Tesco candles—unscented, white, the kind people leave at shrines. She keeps one in her fist like a flare. Tam isn’t here. Tam is tubes and beeping, or maybe nothing. We don’t know. We didn’t visit today because none of us knew what to say. Instead we’ve come for answers, or at least for somewhere louder than our heads. First thing we find: penny-pusher machine still running, coins stacked like tiny silver graves. Kat slides a 2p in. Nothing happens. She tries another. The machine burps, spits out three coins coated in something sticky. Marla wipes her fingers on her jeans; the smear is reddish-brown. Could be rust. Could be worse. Second thing: a Polaroid wedged behind the air-hockey table. Picture shows the four of us last summer—faces sunburnt, arms around each other. On the white border someone’s scrawled 00:07 in black Sharpie. None of us remember taking it. Third thing: the dummy’s mouth is open wider than it should be. Inside sits a card the size of a bus ticket. Tamsin fishes it out with two fingers. The card is blank except for three red fingerprints in a line—thumb, index, middle—pressed so hard the card is indented. No blood, just the shape of it. Kat’s torch flickers. 3 %. “Let’s not lose our minds over arts and crafts,” she says, but her voice wobbles like the pier boards. Marla lights a candle anyway. Flame bends sideways in the wind sneaking through broken panes. She sets it on the booth ledge. Wax drips; the dummy’s plastic cheek looks like it’s crying. Footsteps behind us. We spin like stupid horror extras. Liam Moffat, hood up, carrier bag clinking. He smells of cider and regret. “Didn’t expect an audience,” he says, sheepish. “You sent the emoji,” Tamsin accuses. Liam raises both hands, bag swinging. “Swear on my nan. Phone’s been dead since Saturday. Charger port’s full of sand.” He shows the screen—cracked, black. Kat plays the torch across his face. Lip split, but not bleeding. “Where’d you get the bust mouth?” “Skateboard. Gravity.” He grins, teeth outlined in Vimto. “Thought you lot were at hospital.” “Thought you were clever,” Marla mutters. Liam shrugs, sits on a Dance Dance Revolution mat that’s peeling at the edges. Bag clinks again. Four cans of Monster, raspberry flavour. He offers. Kat takes one—battery conservation. Tamsin pockets the ring-pull; they collect them like charms. Wind howls through gaps in the boards. Somewhere below, waves slap pylons like slaps on skin. Liam cracks his can. “Heard Tam coded this morning.” We all freeze. “Didn’t,” Kat says, too fast. “Mate at A&E said heart stopped for thirty seconds. They shocked her back.” He sips. “Could be bollocks.” Marla’s candle gutters. Wax runs over her knuckles; she doesn’t flinch. Tamsin’s recorder light blinks. “You dream about it?” they ask Liam. He frowns. “About what?” “The emoji. The sea. Anything.” He scratches his neck. “Had one where my tongue turned into a tampon string. Pulled it, kept pulling, miles of it. Woke up choking.” He laughs, but it sounds like gargling. Kat checks her phone—2 %, one bar. She pockets it. Marla lights a second candle. Flame catches the dummy’s glass eye, reflects three tiny fires. The fingerprints on the card seem deeper now, as if the paper’s still wet. Liam stands abruptly. “I’m off. Storm’s coming. You should too.” He leaves footprints in the sawdust—one normal, one outlined in something darker. We watch until he’s gone, until the chain clanks again. Alone now. The dummy’s mouth clicks shut. No wind, just movement. We all jump. Tamsin flicks the recorder off. “Episode four ends here,” they say to nobody. Kat pockets the card with the fingerprints. “Evidence,” she mutters, like it’s a joke she no longer gets. Marla blows the candles out. Smoke curls up, smells like birthday cake left too long in the oven. As we duck back under the barrier, the pier sign finally loses its F altogether. It flaps away into the dark like a tiny white bird. Behind us, inside, the fortune-teller dummy lights up by itself. Just for a second. Just long enough to spell three red letters that might be H-E-L or might be nothing at all. We don’t look back.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 5 is a strong piece of YA gothic fiction that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through its qualities and failings systematically. VOICE AND PERSPECTIVE: The chapter makes an interesting structural choice—it shifts into first-person plural ("we") rather than the close third-person promised in the plan. This is actually effective for this chapter specifically, creating a hive-mind quality that suits the girls' collective dread and their avoidance of Tam's absence. However, it's a deviation from the stated plan. The voice is consistently teenage without being patronising: "stupid horror extras," "cider and regret," "battery conservation" as a reason to accept a drink. These land well. IMAGERY AND METAPHOR: Most of the imagery earns its place. "Coins stacked like tiny silver graves" is genuinely good—small, precise, not overwrought. "Smoke curls up, smells like birthday cake left too long in the oven" is excellent: domestic, slightly wrong, unsettling without straining. The pier sign losing its F "like a tiny white bird" is perhaps the weakest image—it reaches for poetry and lands somewhere slightly soft. The fortune-teller dummy spelling H-E-L is effective in its ambiguity, though the "might be nothing at all" qualifier is a little safe. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is mostly strong. Liam's tampon-string dream is genuinely disturbing and darkly comic in exactly the right register. "Thought you were clever" from Marla is a good deflection. Kat's "Okay but statistically" voice from the profile doesn't quite appear here—she's more muted than her character suggests. Tamsin's "Episode four: we break into capitalism's corpse" is a bit on-the-nose as a line—it sounds like a writer showing their work rather than a teenager being funny. The "Episode four ends here" callback is better. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: Marla's candle-holding, her not flinching at wax burns, her compulsive lighting of a second candle—all consistent with her profile. Tamsin's recorder is well-used. Kat's fact-checking instinct shows in "Evidence." Liam is a good addition—his Vimto-bloody mouth, his cider, his casual delivery of devastating news ("Heard Tam coded this morning") all work. The girls' collective freeze at this news is well-handled. PLOT: The chapter advances the story effectively. The Polaroid with 00:07 is a strong detail. The fingerprint card is good. Liam's information about Tam coding is the emotional gut-punch the chapter needed. The chapter plan mentioned a "live call" from Tam where she says "I can taste metal in my sleep"—this doesn't appear, which is a notable omission. The plan also mentioned Liam's lip being bloody and him swearing it's Vimto—this is included and works well. FORCED POETRY: There are a few moments where the writing reaches too hard. "Chain clanks behind us like a bad omen" is a cliché dressed up as atmosphere. "THREE DROPS, THREE DAYS, THREE CHOICES" on the neon sign feels slightly too convenient, too writerly. The dummy's mouth clicking shut with "No wind, just movement" is effective, but the H-E-L reveal at the end is perhaps one gothic flourish too many in a chapter that's already well-stocked with them. DARKNESS AND LIGHTNESS: The balance is genuinely well-managed. Liam's tampon dream is horrifying and funny simultaneously. The Monster cans being "battery conservation" is a good joke. The chapter earns its darkness through accumulation rather than announcement. COHERENCE: The chapter is coherent and well-paced. The three discoveries (penny-pusher, Polaroid, fingerprint card) give it structure. The Liam interruption is well-timed. AMATEURISH ELEMENTS: The "stupid horror extras" line is slightly self-aware in a way that breaks the spell. The neon sign's "THREE DROPS, THREE DAYS, THREE CHOICES" is a bit too neat, too obviously symbolic. The ending's H-E-L is effective but the "might be nothing at all" hedge is a slight cop-out. Overall, this is a confident, well-executed chapter that mostly delivers on its plan and the broader story's ambitions. Its weaknesses are minor: a few over-reaching images, one missed element from the plan (Tam's live call), and occasional moments where the craft shows too visibly. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 14 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
03:11, Wednesday — A&E, plastic chairs the colour of dried ketchup. The vending machine hums like it’s trying to remember a tune. Every time it drops a Mars bar the clunk echoes down the corridor and someone’s mum flinches. I’m the only one of us awake; the others crashed twenty minutes ago, heads on each other’s shoulders like collapsed tents. Kat’s inhaler lies on her lap like a tiny silver gun. Tamsin’s phone is still recording—red LED pulsing against their hoodie zipper. Marla’s got one of Tam’s old socks balled under her cheek; it’s got a hole in the toe that looks like a mouth. My own eyes burn from the strip lights that never quite stay still. If I stare long enough the ceiling tiles start to move, slow-rolling like surf. I count beeps instead: green monitor next door, drip machine, some kid’s game on maximum brightness. Every third beep matches the pulse in my ears. I lost Tam’s heartbeat somewhere between Radiology and the vending machine that ate Kat’s last pound. “Any change?” It’s Tam’s mum, suddenly beside me with two coffees that taste of cardboard bitterness and powdered milk skin. She’s still in her care-home uniform, name badge cracked: JULIE DOYLE – TEAM LEADER. There’s a tiny smear of blood on the sleeve. Not Tam’s—one of the residents scratched her earlier; she said it like it was nothing. I shake my head. “They won’t let us in.” “They’re ‘optimising’ her, whatever that means.” She tries to smile. It doesn’t reach the mascara smudged underneath. Behind us the automatic doors sigh open and shut, open and shut, like the building is breathing. A porter wheels someone past, blanket pulled up so only white trainers show. I think of Tam’s trainers, still under my bed, caked in yesterday’s sand. I look at my phone: 3 %. Battery saver mode has turned the screen yellow. The group chat is a graveyard of unsent drafts. Marla stirs, mumbles, “Did they say anything?” I tell her, “Still waiting.” She nods like she understands, then tucks the sock tighter under her cheek. Across the corridor a boy in Spiderman pyjamas is trying to do a wheelie with an IV pole. His mum hisses at him to sit down; he ignores her, keeps circling. Each pass makes the pole clack against the floor: clack, clack, clack. I count to twenty, lose track, start again. Kat wakes coughing, fumbles for the inhaler. Two puffs, shaky inhale. “Any crisps?” she croaks. “Machine’s bust,” I say. She nods like that’s the worst thing that’s happened tonight. Tamsin’s recorder blinks once more, then dies. The screen stays red for a second—battery flat. They don’t move, just let their head loll on Kat’s shoulder. In the reflection of the vending machine glass I see four of us, washed out, like the lights are trying to erase us slowly. I stand up. My knees crack louder than the Mars bar clunks. I need to pee but I’m scared if I leave the others will disappear too. I go anyway. Hospital loos smell like bleach left too long. The mirror shows someone who’s not me—hair greasy, hoodie collar stretched, eyes ringed like dirty coins. I splash water on my face. It tastes metallic. I spit into the sink; pink. Didn’t realise I’d bitten my cheek raw. When I come back the vending machine has eaten someone else’s money. I watch a man in hi-vis kick it twice, swear, walk off. The display flickers: £1.00, £0.85, £0.70—counting down like a bomb. A nurse in blue scrubs appears at the doors. She scans the waiting room, spots us, starts walking. My stomach drops into my socks. “Family of Tamara Doyle?” she asks. Julie stands so fast her coffee sloshes onto the lino. “That’s me.” I tug Kat awake. Tamsin blinks, confused. Marla’s eyes snap open, wide, guilty, like she’s been caught dreaming. The nurse’s badge says EMILY – JUNIOR. She’s younger than my cousin. “We’ve stabilised her for now,” she says. “But she’s developed sepsis. She’s on broad-spectrum antibiotics. We’re transferring her to Leeds first thing. You can see her for five minutes before transport.” Five minutes. I feel it shrink inside me like a tissue in water. We follow Emily through swinging doors that smell of hand gel and panic. The corridor is colder; the lights brighter. Tam’s bed is behind a curtain patterned with cartoon fish. She’s pale under the fish, skin waxy, foot propped on pillows like some awful trophy. IV lines snake into her arm; one bag is clear, one the colour of Ribena gone off. Her eyes flick open when we shuffle in. She tries a grin. “You lot look like shit warmed up.” Tamsin starts crying before anyone else. Silent tears, just rolling off their chin onto the blanket. Tam’s voice is paper-thin. “Inhaler, Kat. You’re wheezing louder than the drip.” Kat hands it over like it’s sacred. Tam pretends to spray it at her own face. “See? Fixed.” Marla steps closest. “We brought socks.” She produces the blood-stained grey pair like an offering. Tam snorts. “Keep ‘em. Hospital ones have grippy bottoms. Very fetch.” I can’t speak. My throat’s full of static. Tam looks at me. “You started without me?” she says, nodding toward my shorts—small red patch, darker now. I shrug. “Syncing, apparently.” Tam’s fingers twitch toward the IV pump. “Listen. They’re moving me. Leeds is an hour. If I die on the motorway, I want my playlist, not the radio. Someone grab my phone charger.” Julie covers Tam’s hand. “Not dying, love.” Tam rolls her eyes. “Fine. But just in case, delete my search history. And the photos of me pretending to be a lobster.” Emily reappears. “Two minutes.” We all step closer, bumping tubes, wires. Tam grabs my wrist with surprising strength. “Sea’s high tonight,” she whispers. “Don’t go to the pier.” She looks at each of us, eyes huge. “Promise.” We promise. We lie. Transport team arrives with a trolley that looks like a transformer. Tubes are unclipped, clipped again. The fish curtain flaps shut behind them. We’re left in the corridor with our promises ringing like coins dropped into the wishing well nobody believes in. Back in the waiting room the vending machine finally drops a Mars bar. Nobody moves to get it. The wrapper sits on the metal coil, dangling, like bait. Kat’s phone buzzes: 1 %. A single notification. Group chat, unknown sender: 🩸 00:07 The LED above our heads flickers twice, then steadies.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a genuinely accomplished piece of YA fiction that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through its strengths and weaknesses carefully. VOICE AND PERSPECTIVE: The chapter is written in first person, which is a notable deviation from the "close third-person that pin-balls between four sixteen-year-old girls" described in the writing plan. The chapter plan for Chapter 6 doesn't specify a POV character, but the plan overall specifies close third. Here it reads as Marla's first person (the sock detail, the heartbeat counting, the cheek-biting). This is a meaningful departure, though it works well enough on its own terms. The voice is consistent and convincing—teenage without being patronising, scared without being melodramatic. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is one of the chapter's strongest elements. Tam's lines are pitch-perfect: "You lot look like shit warmed up," the lobster photos, the inhaler gag, the playlist demand. These feel genuinely earned rather than performed. The "Syncing, apparently" exchange about periods is quietly devastating and lands exactly as intended. Julie's "Not dying, love" is perfectly ordinary and therefore heartbreaking. The dialogue does real work—it reveals character, advances plot, and carries the tonal balance between comedy and dread. METAPHORS AND IMAGERY: Most of the imagery earns its place. "Heads on each other's shoulders like collapsed tents" is good. "Eyes ringed like dirty coins" works. The vending machine as structural motif (eating money, finally dropping the Mars bar as bait) is well-executed. "Throat full of static" is slightly worn but functional. The fish curtain is a lovely, specific detail. However, "like a tissue in water" for the five minutes shrinking is a bit weak—the simile doesn't quite capture the sensation. "Like bait" at the end is effective. The pink spit in the sink is visceral and earned. TONAL BALANCE: The chapter successfully walks the darkly comic line. The lobster photos, the grippy hospital socks, the inhaler gag—these are genuinely funny moments that don't undercut the fear. This is hard to do and the chapter does it well. The comedy is defensive, as planned. STRUCTURAL ELEMENTS: The chapter plan called for "phones banned; signal dies at reception" and "a vending machine eats Kat's last pound" and "a nurse says Tam might need 'a bigger hospital'" and "Plan dissolves into giggles, then into silence." The midnight Tesco ritual plan is absent—this is a significant omission from the chapter plan. The chapter doesn't include the fairy lights and half-price steak ritual at all, which was meant to be a key beat. The chapter does include the vending machine, the nurse, and the signal issues, but the ritual planning that "dissolves into giggles" is missing. The ending with the group chat notification (🩸 / 00:07) is strong and faithful to the overall arc of the story. COHERENCE: There's a slight confusion about POV—who is "I"? The chapter doesn't name the narrator. Given the sock detail (Marla has Tam's sock) and the heartbeat-counting (Marla's quirk), it's Marla, but this isn't explicit. The chapter plan doesn't specify a narrator for this chapter, which creates mild ambiguity. SEPSIS: The medical detail (sepsis, broad-spectrum antibiotics, transfer to Leeds) is specific and grounding. This is good. WEAKNESSES: The "building is breathing" metaphor for the automatic doors is slightly overused in hospital fiction. "Like a transformer" for the trolley is a nice callback to Tam's character detail (the melted Transformer toy) but might be accidental. The Spiderman pyjamas boy with the IV pole is a good detail but slightly underused—introduced and then dropped. The chapter could be slightly tighter. OVERALL: This is strong work. The voice is consistent, the dialogue is excellent, the tonal balance is achieved, and the ending lands. The main failures are the missing ritual planning scene from the chapter plan and the first-person deviation from the stated close-third approach. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 15 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 12 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
21:33, Wednesday night, sea wall. The wind has teeth tonight. It drags salt across the concrete and spits it into our eyes until everything blurs—lights, faces, the edge of the world. We left the hospital at eight, walked the beach road because the bus never came, and now we’re here because none of us could think where else to go. Tam’s mum texted while we were still on the ward: gone. No capitals, no emoji, no full stop. Just the word hanging in the group chat like a hole. We stand on the wall that holds the North Sea back and feel how small that wall is. Waves smash below, white foam climbing the stones. The tide is higher than the marker painted in 1983; spray reaches the street lamps and fizzles like fireworks. Salt crusts our lips the second we open our mouths. Tamsin’s recorder is dead so they just hold it anyway, thumb still on the button like habit. Kat’s inhaler is in her fist; every third breath she takes a puff, counts, waits. Marla has Tam’s old hoodie on—too big, sleeves soaked to the elbows—and the hood keeps blowing back so her hair whips her face like cables. Nobody speaks for the first three minutes. Then Kat says, quiet: “They said heart stopped again. They shocked her twice.” “Third time’s the charm,” Tamsin answers, then winces at their own voice. Marla is staring at the horizon where the lighthouse beam slices across black water, then vanishes. “We promised her we wouldn’t come here.” “We promised loads of shit,” I say. My voice comes out rough; I haven’t used it since the ward. The group chat scrolls itself in my head: Tam: sync with the moon Tam: if I die Tam: delete my— I open the chat now, thumb hovering. No one has texted since Julie’s single word. Below it, the triple-drop emoji sits untouched, still red, still unread. A gust knocks Kat sideways. She grabs the railing, knuckles white. “We did this,” she says. “Last summer. The Coke can. We said—” “Shut up,” Tamsin snaps. “Not helpful.” But Kat keeps going. “We said the sea could have any one of us. We said it like a joke.” None of us laugh. We were drunk on two-litre cider and the dizzy feeling of being sixteen and immortal. Tam sliced her finger with a craft-knife, we mixed drops with flat cola, swallowed it like communion. Thought we were hilarious. Now the sea is here to collect. A wave crashes so hard the spray spatters our shoes. Marla steps back, clutching her stomach. “I’m still bleeding,” she says. “Four days straight. Tam stopped yesterday.” She laughs, a broken sound. “Bodies are arseholes.” I look down at the water. It’s black except where the pier lights hit it—then it’s red, white, red, like the emoji pulsing. I think about Tam’s foot, the red lines racing up her ankle like roots. I think about the card with three fingerprints, the dummy’s mouth clicking shut. I think about the promise we never meant. Marla pulls out her phone. Battery 7 %. Torch on. She shines it at the wall beneath us. Spray paints the light pink for a second. Carved into the salt-stained concrete, fresh, are three small crosses. No taller than a finger each. Wet. We didn’t carve them. Tamsin crouches, touches one. Fingers come away red. “Not ours,” they whisper. Kat’s phone buzzes once—1 % battery left. Screen lights with a new voice note. The sender is blank. We crowd around, heads together, hair tangling in the wind. We press play. Tam’s voice, hospital-tiny: “…count to three and then the sea…” Static. Then: “…your turn…” The note ends. Screen goes black. Marla’s torch flickers, dies. We’re left with just the lighthouse beam sweeping past, touching us, moving on. I feel the group chat open in my pocket without me touching it. Phantom buzz. When I look, there’s a single draft already typed: 🩸🩸🩸 Cursor blinking, no sender, no send button. Wind howls again. Spray hits my face; it tastes of pennies and petrol. Kat starts coughing, deep hacks that sound like the sea inside her lungs. Tamsin grips the railing so hard the rust flakes off in their palms. Marla does the only thing left to do. She opens her contacts, scrolls to a number we don’t recognise—saved earlier as “Leeds Girl Field Trip.” The cursor hovers. “Don’t,” Tamsin says. “Not yet.” But Marla’s thumb is already trembling. From far out on the water comes a sound that isn’t wind or wave—a low, rhythmic thud, like a heart amplified. The pier lights go out all at once. Darkness swallows the amusement arcade, the shuttered chip shops, the whole town behind us. Only the group-chat cursor keeps blinking. I realise we’re not four anymore. We’re three. The sea is higher now, kissing the bottom of the wall. My trainers are soaked. Salt stings the cuts I didn’t know I had. Kat says, voice barely there: “What if we just… walk in?” Nobody answers. The thud gets louder. Closer. The lighthouse beam cuts across us one last time, and in that second I see our shadows on the concrete—three shadows, not four—stretching long and thin toward the water. Marla lowers the phone. The draft stays unsent. The cursor keeps blinking. We stand there, wind and salt and the smell of our own fear, waiting for something to happen. It does.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is doing a great deal right, and some things that warrant scrutiny. VOICE AND PERSPECTIVE: The chapter opens in close third-person but then slips into first-person ("My voice comes out rough," "I feel the group chat open in my pocket," "I realise we're not four anymore"). This is a significant technical inconsistency. The plan specifies "close third-person that pin-balls between four sixteen-year-old girls," but the chapter settles into an unidentified first-person narrator for long stretches. This is either a deliberate choice that isn't signalled clearly enough, or a lapse. Given the plan's stated intent, it reads as a lapse. The "I" is never identified, which creates confusion rather than productive ambiguity. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is largely effective and appropriately sparse. "Third time's the charm" followed by "then winces at their own voice" is a strong moment—the joke that fails mid-air, exactly as the plan demands. Kat's "We did this" speech is functional but slightly expository. The dialogue-to-silence ratio is well-judged for the emotional register. IMAGERY AND METAPHOR: Most of the imagery earns its place. "The word hanging in the group chat like a hole" is genuinely good. "Red lines racing up her ankle like roots" works. "Hair whips her face like cables" is slightly clunky but acceptable. The lighthouse beam "touching us, moving on" is quietly effective. However, "the sea is here to collect" is a bit on-the-nose, and "the smell of our own fear" is a cliché that the rest of the chapter's specificity doesn't deserve. The pier lights going red-white-red "like the emoji pulsing" is a nice structural callback. SUPERNATURAL ELEMENTS: The chapter handles the horror well—the carved crosses, the phantom voice note, the draft that appears without a sender. These are genuinely unsettling and feel earned within the story's logic. The "thud like a heart amplified" is slightly overwrought but the pier lights going out saves it. PLAN ADHERENCE: The plan calls for: Tam's mum texting "gone," the girls arriving too late, highest tide since 1983, spray hitting streetlights pink, three phones glowing, group-chat auto-uploading Tam's last voice note with a whispered countdown from three, deleting and restarting the chat, and a tourist girl from Leeds asking for directions causing them to freeze. The chapter delivers most of these but the Leeds girl appears only as a contact in Marla's phone rather than physically asking for directions—a significant deviation that loses the uncanny specificity of the plan. The countdown from three is present but altered. The "delete the chat, restart it, delete again" beat is absent. TONAL CONSISTENCY: The kitchen-sink gothic tone is well-maintained. The comedy is appropriately defensive and brief. The chapter doesn't moralize. CHARACTERS: Tamsin's recorder-holding-out-of-habit is a perfect character detail. Kat's inhaler counting is consistent. Marla in Tam's hoodie is good. The unidentified "I" narrator undermines character specificity. ENDING: "It does." as a final line is bold and effective—a held breath, not a bow, exactly as planned. The three shadows not four is a strong visual. WEAKNESSES: The perspective confusion is the biggest flaw. "The smell of our own fear" is weak. The Leeds girl deviation loses something specific. The chapter occasionally tells us emotional states rather than showing them ("feel how small that wall is" is borderline). The "bodies are arseholes" line is good but the laugh described as "a broken sound" is slightly over-explained. Overall this is a strong chapter with real atmosphere and some genuinely effective horror, undermined by a perspective problem and occasional lapses into cliché. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 13 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 11 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 13 Followed Chapter Plan: 12 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
00:07, Thursday, one week later. Same loft bedroom. The fairy lights are off. The bulb popped last night and no one’s bothered to change it. Outside the skylight the sea is loud, pushing against the bricks like it’s looking for a way in. The town’s electricity has been flickering since sunset; every time it dims the dark feels thicker. We’re three hooded shapes on the floorboards, knees drawn up, phones glowing like coals. The Leeds girl’s username sits in Marla’s contacts: “Ruby (geography yr10)”. No profile picture, just the default Snapchat ghost. Cursor blinks. Empty message box. Carrier bag in the corner holds two things: the blood-stained joggers we never gave back, and the sock from Tam’s foot still crusted stiff. We lit Tesco candles earlier; two guttered out, one drowned in its own wax. The fourth is still burning, flame small and mean. Marla’s thumb hovers. Kat’s breathing counts the seconds. Tamsin’s recorder is dead, but they mouth the words anyway: “Episode eight—finale.” None of us has spoken in fifteen minutes. The air tastes of salt and burnt wick. The group chat sits open above Ruby’s name. Below it: the original triple-drop emoji, still unanswered, still red. Marla scrolls up—screenshot of Tam’s heart-rate spiking, then flatlining at 21:33. The last message Tam ever read was from Julie. One word. We haven’t deleted it. Deleting feels like killing her twice. Wind rattles the window. The candle flickers. Shadows jump across the ceiling like gulls. I clear my throat. “We could send it to nobody. Let the thread die.” Kat shakes her head once, sharp. “Then it ends with us. I’m not ready to be a ghost.” Tamsin whispers, “We already are.” Their voice cracks on the last word. I look at the screen. 00:06. The clock rolls. 00:07. The exact minute the first emoji arrived a week ago. Marla’s thumb twitches. The cursor jumps, types by itself: 🩸 She doesn’t move. Another blink, another drop appears. 🩸🩸 Third drop. 🩸🩸🩸 No sender. No send button. Just the words, typed, unsent, waiting. The candle flares, a single pop of blue at the base. Wax runs like blood. Somewhere below the chippy sign swings loose, squealing on its hinge. Marla looks up. “I dreamed Tam texted me. Said it was my turn.” Kat’s eyes are wet but fierce. “Dreams lie. People lie. Phones don’t.” She pulls up a new draft, identical emoji, but under Ruby’s name this time. “Least we know who it goes to.” Ruby’s last story was a selfie on the sea wall—sunset, peace sign, caption “end of a long day :)”. Leeds school trip staying at the B&B three streets over. We walked past them earlier, hoods up, faces down. They looked happy. Untouched. Tamsin reaches across, covers Marla’s thumb with theirs. “If we do this, we’re the bad guys.” Kat shrugs. “Bad guys live.” I think of Tam’s last voice: *count to three and then the sea*. I think of her foot swollen like a rotten apple. I think of the card with three fingerprints and the dummy’s mouth clicking shut. Marla’s phone buzzes—1 % battery. Screen dims to orange. A low-battery pop-up covers the emoji. She swipes it away. The cursor still blinks. Outside, a wave crashes so hard the attic trembles. The fairy-light string swings though no one’s touched it. Tamsin lets go. “Flip a coin.” Kat: “No coins left. Machine ate them.” Me: “Then we count.” We do. Out loud, slow. One. The candle flame shrinks to a pinhead. Two. The sea roars like a crowd. Three. Marla’s thumb hovers. She doesn’t press send. Instead she tilts the phone so we can all see the screen. The emoji sit there, innocent as stickers. She holds the power button. Slide to power off appears. She hesitates. Thumb over
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This final chapter is a genuinely accomplished piece of YA gothic fiction that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through its strengths and weaknesses carefully. VOICE AND STYLE: The chapter maintains the close, fragmented voice established in the plan. The shift to first person ("I clear my throat," "I think of Tam's last voice") is interesting—it collapses the close-third into something more intimate and destabilizing, which works thematically for a finale. The prose is lean and purposeful: "flame small and mean," "wax runs like blood," "the sea roars like a crowd." These earn their place. The rhythm is controlled. FOLLOWING THE PLAN: The plan called for "Marla on the roof, cracked screen cutting her thumb. One drop of real blood lands on the glass—joins three dried emoji-blood stickers. The new girl's username hovers unsent. Marla's thumb hovers. The sea is loud enough to drown notifications. Final frame is the cursor blinking in an empty message box." The chapter delivers the cursor blinking, the new girl's username, the hovering thumb, and the sea—but relocates the action from the roof to the loft bedroom interior, and the cracked-screen-cutting-thumb detail is replaced by a 1% battery warning. The "one drop of real blood" joining the emoji stickers is absent. This is a meaningful deviation from the plan's most striking image. The chapter also introduces a first-person narrator voice ("I clear my throat," "I think of Tam") that wasn't specified—it's ambiguous whose POV this is, which creates slight incoherence. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is sparse and effective. "Bad guys live" is the standout line—punchy, earned, character-consistent for Kat. "We already are" from Tamsin lands. "Dreams lie. People lie. Phones don't" is slightly too neat, a bit aphoristic, but not egregiously so. The counting sequence (One. Two. Three.) is well-executed. METAPHORS AND POETRY: "Phones glowing like coals" works. "Sock from Tam's foot still crusted stiff" is viscerally effective. "Wax runs like blood" is a touch obvious given the context. "The sea roars like a crowd" is functional but not inspired. "Shadows jump across the ceiling like gulls" is the weakest simile—it's reaching. The fairy-light string swinging untouched is a good gothic touch. Overall, the poetic moments mostly land, with a few misfires. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: Kat's pragmatic ruthlessness ("Bad guys live") is perfectly consistent with her profile. Tamsin's "Episode eight—finale" mouthed silently is a beautiful character detail. Marla's hovering, her inability to commit, her compulsive counting—all consistent. The chapter handles the ensemble well in a small space. EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT: The chapter is genuinely affecting. The detail of not deleting Tam's last message because "deleting feels like killing her twice" is the emotional high point—specific, true, devastating. The countdown structure creates real tension. The ending's refusal to resolve is appropriate and well-executed. COHERENCE: The POV shift to first person is unexplained and slightly jarring. Who is "I"? Marla is named in third person ("Marla's thumb hovers") but also seems to be the "I." This is either a deliberate stylistic choice (the group collapsing into one voice) or a technical error.
