Ends with him crawling toward the patio door, trailing loops of intestine. **7 – “Basement Humidity”** Down the stairs, single bulb swinging. Tigre holds Ellie by the hair, gun to her temple. Negotiation stutters; neither believes the other. Marisol offers herself instead. When he shifts weight, Ellie bites his wrist—cartoon hard. Marisol charges, takes the bullet through the meat of her thigh, buries pruning shears in his neck. They both fall; Tigre bleeds out on a pile of Christmas decorations. **8 – “5:03 a.m.”** Sirens finally wail uphill. Marisol sits on the curb, towel duct-taped around leg, smoking someone else’s cigarette she can’t light because the lighter’s wet with blood. Theo vomits quietly behind the oleander. Ellie keeps repeating the color of the getaway van—green, dirty, one headlight. Marisol nods, files it away, already knowing she’ll have to finish this before the cops do. Sun comes up orange through smog; the house behind them looks like any other on the block, except for the footprints in red.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Marisol Vega **Background** Daughter of a Brownsville truck-stop waitress and an absentee long-haul driver. Enlisted at nineteen to escape credit-card fraud charges—turns out she was good at making people talk, even better at knowing when to stop (which she usually didn’t). Two tours in the Horn of Africa running “field interviews.” Dishonorable discharge after a prisoner died in a shipping container; paperwork says heart failure, autopsy says “seventeen different traumas.” She’s been scrubbing motel showers with bleach ever since, chasing honest money and failing. Took the babysitting gig because the dad, Captain Hensley, once pulled her out of a reprimand and she still doesn’t know why. **Personality & Idiosyncrasies** - Won’t sit with her back to a door; rotates plates in diners so chipped edge faces away. - Keeps a grocery list on her forearm with a Sharpie—adds things she’s already bought so she can cross them off and feel productive. - Chews the inside of her cheek until it bleeds when she lies. - Hates cotton balls; the squeak makes her teeth itch. - Sleeps in stolen hotel slippers because “they’re already broken in.” **Physicality** Five-six, 140 lb, most of it shoulders and callus. Black hair always in a ragged knot, two silver studs in one ear (the other got torn out bar-fighting). A burn scar on her right wrist looks like spilled wax. Moves like someone who expects the floor to lurch—weight on the balls of her feet, elbows tucked. When idle, she drums a silent paradiddle against her thigh. **Motives & Allegiances** Pays restitution to the dead prisoner’s family under a fake name—$200 a month, money order, postmarked El Paso. Protecting the Hensley kids is half penance, half stubborn refusal to let anyone else own her ledger. Owes Captain Hensley a debt she can’t articulate, so settles it in small, violent installments. **Voice Samples** - Spoken, cleaning a skinned knuckle with vodka: “Hurts mean it’s still mine.” - Internal, watching Ellie braid Barbie hair: *Don’t get attached—plastic melts at four hundred degrees.* - Over walkie to Tigre, voice flat: “You want them, walk through me. Bring a mop.” # Theo Hensley **Background** Twelve, homeschooled after getting suspended for “unauthorized penetration testing” of the district’s grading server. Dad taught him BASIC instead of bedtime stories. Mom split years ago; memory is a smell of dryer sheets and airport announcements. Hasn’t slept without night-light code running on his tablet since. **Personality & Idiosyncrasies** - Eats cereal dry, by color—yellow loops first, brown last. - Keeps a ranked list of every lie adults told him; top entry: “The dog went to live on a farm.” - Hums the Windows XP shutdown sound when anxious. - Will argue about anything, then blush if someone agrees. **Physicality** All elbows and knees, wrists too big for his hands. Hair refuses to lay flat; one cowlick stands at attention like antenna. Glasses held together with electrical tape, lenses smeared. Sits cross-legged even on hard floors, ankles going purple. **Motives & Allegiances** Loyal to data and to anyone who doesn’t treat him like a shortcut. Sees Marisol as an adult who forgets to lie; that’s enough. Will risk frying his only laptop if it buys Ellie thirty seconds. **Voice Samples** - Muttered while code scrolling: “Firewall’s a joke—like putting a Band-Aid on a shark bite.” - To Marisol, shaking: “I broke mom’s orchid once and she cried for two days. Don’t let them break Ellie.” # Ellie Hensley **Background** Six, non-verbal until four, now talks in color and sequence. Diagnosed with eidetic memory and perfect pitch—remembers the exact squeak of every cabinet hinge. Has never lost a game of Memory. Believes every grown-up has a theme song; Marisol’s is “low kettle hum.” **Personality & Idiosyncrasies** - Lines up Cheerios by saturation before eating. - Insists socks must match the day’s weather emoji. - Hugs knees and rocks when sirens wail. - Names the house Roomba “Slowpoke” and yells at it like a dog. **Physicality** Tiny, 38 lb, hair in two uneven puffs because she won’t sit still for the second elastic. Always barefoot indoors; soles are gray and tough as leather. Eyes dart like she’s reading subtitles no one else sees. **Motives & Allegiances** Needs patterns to feel safe; Marisol’s routines became one. Will repeat a stranger’s phone number six months later if it keeps Ellie calm. Knows her dad is in “a long meeting”—codes it as “gray tie day.” **Voice Samples** - Whispered to her Barbie: “Marisol smells like metal and oranges. Metal means thunder.” - After Tigre grabs her: “His watch beeps every thirty seconds. I count. One-Mississippi, two—” # Tigre (Ernesto Saldaña) **Background** Juárez cartel lieutenant, former Mexican Army corporal dishoncharged for selling ammo piecemeal. Spent nineteen months in a black site where Marisol kept him awake singing “Rock-a-bye Baby” off-key. Returned home to find his wife remarried, kids calling another man “papá.” Debt and rage metastasized into meticulous cruelty. **Personality & Idiosyncrasies** - Writes grocery lists in Excel on his phone—eggs, duct tape, bleach. - Always wears two watches: one set to Juárez time, one to wherever his target sleeps. - Chews toothpicks until they splinter, spitting splinters like sunflower husks. - Cannot stand the sound of lullabies; punches the car radio when they come on. **Physicality** Medium height, thick in the middle, veins like blue yarn on forearms. Surgical scar under left eye from a botched prison shank. Walks heel-first, boots polished but cracked. Keeps pink child-size backpack slung over shoulder—used to carry ransom toys, now empty and ominous. **Motives & Allegiances** Owes the cartel a spectacular gesture to stay alive. Wants Marisol to *see* what she made. Loyalty is transactional; if the job goes sideways, he’ll trade the kids for his own exit. **Voice Samples** - Over

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The bowl hit the sink with a clatter that made Ellie flinch. Marisol muttered a quick sorry and rinsed broccoli florets off porcelain while the kid stared at her socks—today’s pair was storm-cloud gray, which meant rain emoji even though the sky outside the window was clear. That was Ellie logic, and Marisol had learned not to argue. “Bedtime in fifteen,” she called over her shoulder to the living room. Theo didn’t answer, thumbs tapping tablet glass like he was typing the end of the world. The blue glow across his face made him look underwater. Marisol dried her hands on the front of the oversized T-shirt she’d stolen from Captain Hensley’s laundry. It read NAVY 10K FUN RUN 2017, faded to the color of dishwater. The shirt hung past her hips, flapping against pajama pants patterned with tiny tacos that Ellie had picked out last week at Target. The kid had insisted they matched Marisol’s “theme song,” whatever that meant. On the counter, her phone buzzed. Unknown Number. She let it ring once, twice, then stabbed the red button. Third call like that since six. No voicemail, no text. Just dead air and the taste of pennies in her mouth. She pushed the chair under the front doorknob out of habit—one of those muscle-memory things that made landlords raise eyebrows. The chair was a flimsy IKEA thing, white veneer chipped at the corners, but it wedged tight between knob and hardwood. Enough to slow someone down. Enough to buy thirty seconds. Ellie tugged her sleeve. “Bath now?” “After story,” Marisol said. Her voice came out rougher than she meant; she cleared it like clearing a jammed round. Ellie nodded solemnly and padded toward the stairs, socks sliding on the glossy floor. Upstairs smelled like kid—shampoo and grape lip balm and that warm skin scent that made Marisol’s chest do a weird hitch every damn time. She tucked Ellie in first, pulling the blanket to her chin. The six-year-old’s eyes were wide, fixed on the ceiling fan like it might start spelling secrets. Marisol pulled the book from the shelf. *Goodnight Moon*. She hated it—too quiet, too final—but Ellie liked the rhythm. She read in a flat monotone, words clipped. When she got to the quiet old lady whispering hush, her voice cracked. She pretended it was just dry air. Theo appeared in the doorway, glasses fogged from screen glare. “You know the dad’s servers are wide open, right? I could patch that in five minutes.” “Not tonight, Tech Support,” Marisol said. She set the book down and stood. The floorboard outside Ellie’s room creaked under her weight—always the same spot. She’d mapped the house the first week, catalogued every groan of timber the way other people memorize exit rows. Theo followed her to his room, complaining that eight-thirty was “a war crime against preteens.” She nudged him toward the bed with two fingers between his shoulder blades. He collapsed cross-legged on the duvet, tablet still glowing like a campfire he couldn’t leave. “Sleep or I confiscate the power cord,” she said. It came out softer than she wanted. Theo rolled his eyes but set the tablet on the nightstand. The screen went dark, and for a second the room felt cave-black. Marisol stood in the hall, listening. House sounds: refrigerator hum, Ellie’s soft breathing, Theo shifting under covers. Then the distant grind of tires on gravel. She tilted her head—too late for deliveries, too early for the Hensleys’ flight back tomorrow. She moved to the window at the top of the stairs. The road below curved like a snake, one streetlamp bleeding halos into fog. A black SUV idled at the foot of the driveway, headlights off, engine running. Two figures silhouetted in the front seat. A third leaning against the hood, phone to his ear. Marisol’s stomach went tight. She reached for the switchblade she wasn’t supposed to carry in a house with kids—found the taco pajama pocket empty except for a lint-covered mint. Back in the hall, she opened the linen closet, felt along the top shelf until her fingers closed on duct tape and a short aluminum flashlight heavy enough to crack bone. She tucked both into her waistband, pulled the pajama shirt over them like a bad magic trick. Downstairs, she killed the living-room lamp. Shadows swallowed the furniture. She crouched beside the couch, lifted the rug, and slid her fingers along the hardwood seam until she found the loose plank. Inside: a canvas pouch holding a .38 revolver wrapped in a dish towel. Captain Hensley had called it “insurance.” She called it “plan C.” The pouch went into the taco waistband too. It bulged like she was shoplifting produce. Back at the window, the SUV was moving—slow roll up the incline, headlights still dark. Crunch of gravel under tires. Close enough now she could see the passenger’s outline raise something long and rectangular. Rifle silhouette. She stepped back, heart kicking against ribs. Downstairs, the chair under the doorknob looked suddenly pathetic. She thought of Ellie’s storm socks, Theo’s broken glasses, the broccoli still wilting in the sink. Marisol exhaled through her teeth. “Okay, then,” she whispered to the empty hallway. “Clock’s running.” She padded to the kids’ rooms, cracked each door just enough to see chests rising and falling. Ellie had thrown off the blanket; Theo had already reclaimed the tablet, blue glow under his chin. She left them there for the moment. Went back downstairs, barefoot, silent on the cold tiles. The front door’s deadbolt slid home with a soft click. Outside, the SUV stopped. Engine cut. Doors opened. Boots on gravel. Marisol pressed her back to the wall beside the door, revolver grip sweaty against palm. She could hear her own pulse in her ears—steady, counting down. From upstairs, a small voice carried: “Marisol? I can’t find Slowpoke.” She closed her eyes. “Be right there, kid.” Boots on the porch now. Four of them, maybe five. A low laugh, someone testing the handle. Marisol thumbed back the hammer. The taco pants rode low on her hips, elastic stretched by metal and duct tape. She felt the faint tremor in her hand—not fear, not yet. Just the body revving up. The chair under the knob gave a warning creak. She smiled, small and sharp. “Come on in,” she whispered to the dark. “Door’s open.”
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong opening chapter that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through the key elements. **Voice and Style** The writing successfully executes the promised style: short sentences during action, longer ones during domestic calm. The prose is controlled and purposeful. "She cleared it like clearing a jammed round" is a good character-revealing simile—it earns its place. The taco pajama pants as a recurring motif works well, grounding the absurdity of the situation in domestic reality. The voice is consistent and credible throughout. **Character Work** Marisol comes through clearly: the muscle-memory chair wedge, the loose floorboard mapping, the hidden revolver under the rug—all show rather than tell her background. The "plan C" designation implies plans A and B exist, which is a nice touch. Her voice cracking on "hush" in Goodnight Moon is genuinely affecting. Theo and Ellie are rendered efficiently—Theo's tablet glow, Ellie's storm-cloud socks, "Slowpoke" the Roomba—these details are consistent with their profiles and economically deployed. **Dialogue** The dialogue is sparse but functional. "Not tonight, Tech Support" is a bit flat but not egregious. "Be right there, kid" lands well. The final whispered "Come on in" risks the movie-poster quality the plan explicitly wanted to avoid—it's the one moment where the writing reaches for cool and almost tips into cliché. **Metaphors and Poetry** Most metaphors land: "thumbs tapping tablet glass like he was typing the end of the world" is slightly overwrought but forgivable. "The road below curved like a snake" is generic. "Bleeding halos into fog" is competent. The "shoplifting produce" line for the bulging waistband is genuinely funny and earned. No serious purple prose offenses. **Plot and Tension** The chapter builds tension effectively. The unknown calls, the SUV, the rifle silhouette—the escalation is well-paced. The domestic details (broccoli, Goodnight Moon, Ellie's socks) create genuine stakes. The chapter ends exactly where the plan says it should, with boots on the porch and the chair creaking. **Weaknesses** The final whispered line "Come on in. Door's open" is the chapter's weakest moment—it's exactly the kind of line that wants to be quoted, which the plan explicitly warned against. The "taste of pennies" for anxiety is a well-worn cliché. "Heart kicking against ribs" is similarly familiar. The chapter is perhaps slightly too polished—the plan called for sentences that run on "when panic fogs her head," but the prose remains controlled throughout, even during the tense final sequence. The plan's promise of stylistic variation isn't fully realized yet. **Faithfulness to Plan** The chapter hits all its planned beats: domestic chaos, unknown caller, chair under knob, headlights cutting the driveway. The Lego detail from the plan is absent, which is a minor miss. The chapter ends slightly past where the plan indicated (the plan ends with headlights; the chapter extends to boots on the porch), but this is an improvement, not a failure. **Overall** This is confident, competent genre writing with genuine craft. It's not a masterpiece—some clichés slip through, the final line undercuts the plan's stated goals, and the stylistic variation promised isn't fully present—but it's well above average and successfully establishes character, stakes, and tension. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The door exploded inward, splintering the IKEA chair into white kindling. A boot—steel-toed, dusty—followed through the wreckage, then the rest of the man: thick shoulders, black hoodie, cheap ski mask. He carried a short-barrel shotgun like it weighed nothing. Marisol was already moving. She dropped low behind the couch as the second boot crossed the threshold. Behind the first guy, two more shapes spilled in, fanning, flashlights crisscrossing the living room in jittery white knives. “Clear!” the first one yelled, voice muffled by knit wool. She could hear the fourth man still outside—engine ticking, radio spitting Spanish too fast for her to catch. She tasted metal and broccoli. Ellie’s voice floated down the stairs: “Marisol?” Footsteps froze. One light snapped toward the banister. “Kid,” hoodie muttered. “Shit.” Marisol stood up behind the couch like she was stretching after a long movie. The .38 hung loose in her right hand; flashlight filled her left. She didn’t aim—just flicked the torch into hoodie’s eyes and spoke flat. “Wrong house.” He swung the shotgun. She shot the flashlight instead of him—shattered lens, blinding white burst that made him flinch. She was already moving, vaulting the couch, shoulder driving into his ribs. They crashed into the coffee table; magazines and half a Lego space station exploded under them. The shotgun roared into the ceiling, raining plaster. One of the other men shouted, “¡Bájala!” but the word stretched into a shriek when she drove the jagged flashlight barrel into his thigh. It wasn’t deep, but it bought her a second. Second man lunged. She felt his fist glance off her ear, ringing it like a bell. She spun, found the revolver now pressed under his chin. “Back up,” she said. He did—one step, two—until his heel caught the rug and he went down hard. The gun barked once, loud in the hallway, and the sound swallowed everything. For a heartbeat nobody moved. Then the smell hit: cordite, copper, and something worse leaking from the guy on his back. Third man was already up the stairs, boots hammering. Marisol’s heart kicked sideways. She hurdled the body, pajama pants catching on the dead man’s belt buckle, ripping at the seam. Taco print flapped around her knee as she took the stairs two at a time. Halfway up she met Ellie standing in unicorn pajamas, thumb in mouth, eyes huge. “Back,” Marisol hissed, shoving the kid behind her. She leveled the revolver up the stairwell. The third intruder—blue windbreaker, no mask—had Theo by the hood. The boy’s glasses hung off one ear; the man’s pistol jammed under his chin hard enough to leave half-moon dents. “Drop it,” windbreaker said. His accent was soft, Texas stretched thin. “I’ll paint the wall.” Marisol’s finger tightened. She could see Theo’s eyes behind cracked lenses: not crying, just wide and calculating like he was debugging code. Ellie’s small hand found the back of her pajama shirt and twisted the fabric. Windbreaker took one step down. “Three seconds.” One: Marisol’s pulse thudded. Two: She flicked her gaze left—hall bathroom door ajar, white tile gleaming. Three: She shot the wall instead of him. Plaster exploded. Windbreaker flinched sideways, shielding his face. In the half-second of confusion Marisol lunged up two more steps, grabbed Theo by the collar and yanked. Boy and captor tumbled together, a tangle of limbs and yells. Revolver skittered across the landing. They rolled into the bathroom. Marisol kicked the door shut with her heel, flipped the lock just as the first bullet punched through the cheap hollow core. Splinters rained onto Theo’s hair. He whispered, “There’s a window.” “First-floor roof,” she answered, already moving. She hauled the shower curtain down—plastic rings pinged off tile—and wrapped it around her fist. The window was small, painted shut. She smashed the pane with her wrapped hand, felt glass bite through plastic into skin. Outside, cold air slapped her face. A steep drop to the garage roof, then the ground. She glanced back—Theo’s lip bled where the gun barrel had scraped. Ellie peeked around the doorframe downstairs, thumb still in mouth. Footsteps pounded back down the hall. Marisol boosted Theo through first. He hit the shingles with a grunt. She followed, belly scraping sill, taco pants snagging on a shard. For a moment she hung half-in, half-out, blood running down her forearm. Inside the bathroom, the door burst open. She let go and dropped. Hit the garage roof hard, rolled, felt something pop in her left ankle. Theo was already crawling toward the edge. Below, the fourth man stood by the SUV, rifle up, scanning. She grabbed Theo’s hoodie. “Other way.” They crab-walked across the roof, tiles shifting under knees. Behind them, windbreaker appeared in the window, handgun raised. A muzzle flash lit his face—angry, young, barely older than Marisol had been in that shipping container. The bullet whanged off the gutter. Marisol rolled off the far edge, dragging Theo with her. They landed in the side flowerbed—damp soil, crushed lavender. The smell was sudden, ridiculous. She pulled the kids behind the AC unit just as headlights flared alive at the front of the house. Engine revved. Tires spun gravel. In her pocket the walkie crackled, the voice she remembered too well: “Where’s my package, Maricruz?” She looked at Theo’s bleeding lip, at Ellie’s unicorn socks now soaked with dew and darker things. “Clock’s still running,” she muttered, and started counting shots left in the revolver: four.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter delivers a kinetic, well-controlled action sequence that largely succeeds on its own terms. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. STRENGTHS: The prose rhythm is genuinely accomplished. Short sentences during action ("She dropped low behind the couch"), longer constructions when chaos accumulates—this mirrors the stated intention in the writing plan. The sentence-level craft is mostly clean and purposeful. "Taco print flapped around her knee" is a small, perfect detail that grounds the absurdity of the situation without winking at the reader. The violence is appropriately clumsy and loud, as planned. The shotgun going into the ceiling, the revolver skittering across the landing, the ankle popping on landing—these are the unglamorous mechanics of real physical struggle, not movie choreography. The author avoids the heroic one-liner trap almost entirely. Character consistency is strong. Marisol moves on the balls of her feet, makes tactical decisions under pressure, and her moral complexity is hinted at in the detail about the young intruder's face being "barely older than Marisol had been in that shipping container." That's a well-placed, earned moment of moral weight without being heavy-handed. Ellie's "thumb in mouth, eyes huge" and Theo's glasses hanging off one ear are efficient, character-consistent details. Theo's "not crying, just wide and calculating like he was debugging code" is excellent—it's specific to his profile without being labored. The chapter plan called for: front door kicked in, three men, Marisol shoving kids into pantry, cast-iron skillet, pocketing the walkie. The actual chapter diverges significantly—no pantry, no skillet, the .38 appears without setup, and the walkie is already in her pocket without explanation of how she got it. This is a notable deviation from the plan, though the chapter works on its own terms. WEAKNESSES: The .38 appearing in Marisol's hand is unexplained. The plan had her grabbing a cast-iron skillet; instead she has a revolver from the start. This creates a coherence question—where did it come from? The chapter doesn't establish she was armed going in. "She tasted metal and broccoli" is a good line, but "tasted metal" is a well-worn fear/adrenaline cliché. Similarly, "ringing it like a bell" for the ear hit is stock. The walkie appearing in her pocket at the end without any scene of her taking it from a body is a continuity gap. The plan specified she pockets it from an intruder; here it just materializes. "Flashlights crisscrossing the living room in jittery white knives" is the chapter's most strained metaphor—knives don't criss-cross in the way flashlight beams do, and the image doesn't quite cohere visually. The dialogue is sparse but functional. "Wrong house" and "Back up" are appropriately flat. "I'll paint the wall" is a decent threat. The dialogue doesn't particularly distinguish itself but avoids the movie-poster quality the plan warned against. The ending with Tigre's voice on the walkie calling her "Maricruz" (not her name—is this intentional misdirection or an error?) is intriguing but slightly confusing. If intentional, it's a nice detail suggesting he's taunting her with a wrong name; if an error, it's a continuity problem. The lavender smell detail ("sudden, ridiculous") is one of the chapter's best moments—exactly the kind of sensory intrusion that makes violence feel real rather than cinematic. OVERALL: A competent-to-good action chapter with strong prose rhythm, good character consistency, and some genuine craft. The deviation from the chapter plan (no skillet, no pantry, unexplained revolver, walkie appearing without acquisition) is the main structural weakness. The violence is appropriately unglamorous. A few clichés and one strained metaphor, but nothing that seriously undermines the work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 12 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 9 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The pantry smelled like rice and panic. Marisol shoved the kids inside, shoulder bumping a shelf that rattled boxes of gluten-free crackers. Theo wedged himself between a Costco sack of basmati and the vacuum sealer Ellie’s mom bought during an infomercial phase. Ellie curled fetal, hugging a can of black beans like it was a teddy bear. “Stay inside the tape,” Marisol said, scraping a strip of duct tape across the threshold. It was crooked; her hands shook just enough for the adhesive to stick to her thumb. “Do not cross this line. Not for anything.” Theo nodded, glasses fogged. His tablet glowed cold on his lap, screen cracked from the rooftop jump. “I can loop the cameras. Just need Wi-Fi.” “It’s down,” she said. The router had died with the first gunshot. She didn’t bother adding it was probably shot. Ellie whispered, “Slowpoke is under the couch.” The robot vacuum had trundled off somewhere, maybe dead too. Marisol didn’t have time to mourn appliances. She shut the pantry door and left them darkness. Back in the hallway she pressed her cheek to the wall, feeling the house breathe. Somewhere the HVAC clicked on—a low mechanical sigh that used to comfort her and now sounded like a death rattle. The walkie on the dead man’s belt had gone quiet. She unclipped it, thumb hovering over transmit. Didn’t press. Instead she pried the battery cover, slipped the 9-volt into her pocket, and let the plastic shell clatter to the tile. Kitchen next. She moved low, ankle screaming from the garage roof drop. The revolver felt heavier with every step—four rounds left, no spare speed-loaders. She needed tools she could swing or stab. Knife block first: six blades, all dull suburban stainless. She grabbed the bread knife—serrated, nine inches, handle sticky from jam. Second drawer: rubber bands, flashlight batteries, a half-used roll of aluminum foil. She stuffed foil into her waistband like medieval armor. Third drawer: matchbooks from bars she’d never visited, a Zippo with a naked-lady decal worn smooth. She flicked the Zippo. Flame caught on the first try. Good omen or bad, she wasn’t sure. The pantry door creaked open an inch. Theo’s eye appeared. “They cut the landline. Fiber too.” “Back inside the tape.” She nudged the door shut with her knee. She needed noise, distraction. The smart fridge had a speaker—she’d seen Theo pair it once to play Minecraft parodies. She tapped the screen. Locked. Behind it, the electrical panel sat recessed in drywall. She jammed the bread knife tip under the cover plate and pried. Breakers stared back like judgmental little switches. Garage roof again—no, ankle too loud. Upstairs bathroom window—maybe. She needed height and sightlines. Her brain ran old maps: which floorboard squeaked, which vent carried sound. She’d measured the house in nights of insomnia, pacing while the kids slept. A board creaked overhead. Not hers. Someone was moving in the master bedroom. She killed the kitchen lights. Darkness swallowed everything except the oven clock—3:14 a.m. glow the color of sick. Footsteps above: slow, deliberate. Then a softer step—socked, maybe. Two of them left, hunting. She crouched, slid the lower cabinet open. Inside, wedged behind cereal boxes, was the vacuum sealer Theo’s mom used once for freezer meals. Heavy plastic roll loaded. Marisol yanked the plug free, coiled the cord around her forearm like garrote wire. Another sound—metal on wood. Someone checking the gun safe in the closet. Empty since the captain took his Glock to the safe house. She’d left the safe open purposely; let them waste time. She needed to move. She crawled to the living room, belly on cool tile, revolver leading. Couch blocked view to the stairs. She slid behind it, pressed her back to the baseboard. From here she could see the front door hanging off one hinge, starlight spilling inside like spilled milk. The walkie battery in her pocket felt warm. She rolled it between fingers—then jammed it into the Zippo’s flame chamber. Plastic blistered, hissed. She wrapped the scorched mess in foil, twisted tight. Improvised flash-bang; might buy three seconds, maybe two. A voice drifted down the stairs—Tigre, soft and conversational. “I can smell your shampoo, Maricruz. Same brand from the box.” Her scalp prickled. Coconut and hibiscus. Generic dollar-store bottle. She remembered the box: Mogadishu, 2016, shipping container humming with heat. She’d hummed that stupid lullaby because her MP3 had died. He’d begged her to stop. She bit the inside of her cheek until she tasted iron. Footsteps started down. She could track them by ear: third stair creak, fifth silent, seventh groaned. She counted. When the seventh whined, she flicked the Zippo, touched flame to foil-wrapped battery, and lobbed it up the stairwell. POP—sharp and white. Not loud, but enough. She burst around the couch, revolver up. Silhouette staggered, hand over eyes. She fired once into the dark, saw the muzzle flash paint the banister red. Someone yelled; return fire splintered drywall by her ear. She ducked back. Three rounds left. From the pantry, a muffled beep—Theo’s tablet dying. The sound was tiny, but in the hush it felt like a scream. Footsteps pivoted, heading kitchenward. Marisol was already moving. She vaulted the couch again, ankle screaming, and slipped into the laundry room. Dryer door open—perfect. She tossed the vacuum-sealer cord inside, slammed the door, and hit start. Drum thumped like boots upstairs. She ducked out the other side, into the narrow hallway that led to the garage access. She needed height. The garage attic hatch hung overhead, pull-string dangling. She jumped, caught it, yanked. Ladder unfolded with a metallic shriek. She climbed, every rung a fresh spike through her ankle. Attic was hot, close, smelled of mouse droppings and Christmas. She crawled between rafters, insulation itching through taco fabric. A small octagonal window looked down on the driveway where the SUV sat empty now. Two figures moved across the lawn, flashlights sweeping. She unwrapped the aluminum foil from her fist, tore a strip, and pressed it against the windowpane. Reflected light blinked—a crude signal. Let them think she was outside. Buy minutes. Back in the dark, she breathed through her mouth to keep the attic dust from triggering a cough. On the floorboards below, someone was walking the exact path she’d paced on so many sleepless nights. She pictured the house plan in her head: kids boxed in pantry, duct tape crooked. Vacuum sealer thumping like heartbeat. Bread knife somewhere on the kitchen floor, sticky with someone else’s jam. Somewhere below, Tigre’s voice floated again—closer now, almost conversational. “House rules, Maricruz. You taught me those.” She pressed her cheek to the hot glass and whispered to the night, “Rule one: never be where they expect.”
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 3 is a tightly constructed piece of survival fiction that largely delivers on the writing plan's promises. Let me examine it critically across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The chapter's greatest achievement is its sensory specificity. Details like "Costco sack of basmati," "vacuum sealer Ellie's mom bought during an infomercial phase," and "matchbooks from bars she'd never visited" feel genuinely observed rather than generic thriller furniture. These aren't decorative—they establish the domestic space being violated and Marisol's tactical mind cataloguing everything. The pacing is well-managed. Short declarative sentences during action ("Kitchen next. She moved low, ankle screaming.") contrast with slightly longer constructions during the quieter moments of planning. This mirrors the writing plan's stated intention about sentence rhythm. Marisol's character is consistent with her profile. The paradiddle-drumming, the tactical inventory of the house, the cheek-biting when she hears Tigre's voice—these align with the established character. Her improvised flash-bang from a battery and foil is the kind of clumsy, plausible ingenuity the plan called for rather than action-hero slickness. The Tigre moment is the chapter's emotional core and it lands. "Same brand from the box" and the Mogadishu callback create genuine dread. The coconut-hibiscus shampoo detail is specific enough to feel real. The iron taste from biting her cheek is a nice character-consistent beat. Ellie's "Slowpoke is under the couch" is perfectly in character—the child's concern for the Roomba amid chaos is both darkly funny and emotionally true without being played for laughs. WEAKNESSES: The chapter plan specified: "Theo tries 911; no bars. Smart-hub keeps cheerfully announcing 'BACK DOOR AJAR.' Marisol strips the dead man for car keys, finds a photo of his kids instead, shoves it back in disgust. She herds the children upstairs; Ellie's socks slip on the hardwood, squeak like terrified mice." The chapter deviates significantly from this plan. The smart-hub announcement is absent. The dead man's photo of his kids—a morally resonant beat that was explicitly planned—is entirely missing. The children are not herded upstairs; they remain in the pantry. Ellie's sock-slip moment is gone. These aren't minor omissions; the photo of the dead man's kids was meant to open Marisol's moral ledger, which is a stated thematic priority. Some metaphors don't quite land. "Starlight spilling inside like spilled milk" is weak—the repetition of "spill" feels accidental rather than intentional, and the simile is too gentle for the context. "Oven clock—3:14 a.m. glow the color of sick" is trying hard but "color of sick" is vague where it should be precise. "Taco fabric" for the pajama pants is a jarring tonal shift that reads as a continuity error rather than a deliberate choice. "Novelty pajama pants" from the plan become "taco fabric" here—this is fine, but the transition is abrupt and unexplained. The ending line—"Rule one: never be where they expect"—is exactly the kind of would-be quotable line the writing plan explicitly said to avoid. It's not egregious, but it's a slip. The chapter also introduces Tigre's voice on the stairs, which feels slightly premature given the plan places his major confrontation in Chapter 7. His presence here is atmospheric but risks deflating the later reveal. The dialogue is sparse but functional. Theo's "I can loop the cameras. Just need Wi-Fi" is efficient. The pantry exchange is appropriately clipped. OVERALL: A competent, often impressive chapter that captures the intended voice and maintains tension, but it deviates from its own plan in ways that cost it some moral complexity, and contains a few metaphors that don't earn their keep. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 15 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 9 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The garage door motor whined like a dying cat. Marisol had yanked the red emergency cord so the chain hung slack, then spliced the opener’s wires with Theo’s cracked phone charger and a nine-volt. When the button on the laundry-room wall got hammered, the whole rig snapped. Cartel guy number three—windbreaker now splattered with drywall dust—stepped under the door just as it dropped. Thirty pounds of aluminum slammed across his forearm. Bone cracked loud as a green branch. He screamed something that started in Spanish and ended in a high, animal squeal. Marisol was already there. She came off the attic ladder like a cat off a hot tin roof, ankle throbbing but holding. One bare foot landed on a bag of lawn fertilizer; the plastic split and chemical stink puffed up. She swung the bread knife in a clumsy arc. The serrated edge bit cloth and skin, tore a flap from windbreaker’s biceps. Blood dotted the dusty concrete in fat, dark coins. He backhanded her with the good arm. Stars exploded behind her eyes. She sprawled against the workbench, knocking jars of nails to the floor. Tiny brass screws pinged everywhere like hail. “Puta—” He lunged. She grabbed the first thing her fingers found: a plastic gas can, quarter full. Swung it two-handed. The nozzle caught him square in the teeth. Gasoline sloshed across both of them, cold and sharp. He reeled, spitting pink. Marisol flicked the Zippo open with her thumb. The flame winked blue. “Back up,” she said. He did. One step, two, until his shoulders hit the closed garage door. Behind him, the broken arm hung crooked, fingers twitching like busted antennae. The lighter hissed. She didn’t throw it—couldn’t risk fire this close to the house with kids inside. Instead she tossed the empty can at his feet, watched his eyes track it. Used the second to snatch a claw hammer from the pegboard. “Where’s your boss?” she asked. Windbreaker spat blood. “Close enough to hear you beg.” Marisol swung. The hammer’s claw tore a furrow across his thigh. He folded with a grunt. She brought the handle down on the wrist of his good arm—heard small bones crunch. The pistol he’d been reaching for skittered under the Toyota. She kicked him once, hard, in the ribs. He curled around the sound of his own breathing. “Garage physics,” she muttered. “Lever, fulcrum, pain.” From inside the house came the muffled thump of the vacuum sealer shutting off. Silence afterward, too thick. She pressed her back to the garage’s side door, cracked it two inches. Night air cut through gasoline and sweat. Front lawn lit by the neighbor’s motion light—cheap sodium orange. Two flashlights swept in opposite arcs. Tigre’s voice rode the dark, conversational as a phone call. “Check the side. She’s small, but she bites.” Marisol ducked back. Windbreaker groaned, tried to crawl. She knelt long enough to zip-tie his wrists with the cord she’d stripped from the vacuum. The plastic bit deep; his pulse jumped against it like a trapped moth. She gagged him with his own bandana, gasoline taste making him gag harder. Then she was moving again, through the laundry room, past the thumping dryer. She killed the breaker for the kitchen lights—one-handed flick in the dark. House dropped to shadows and the green glow of oven digits: 3:27. She needed noise, chaos. Needed them spread thin. The smart fridge stood humming like a guilty witness. She yanked the ice-maker line from the wall, copper tube spitting cold water across tile. It hissed like a snake. She jammed the tube into the electrical outlet behind the fridge. Sparks popped, acrid smoke. Breaker tripped, half the house lights dying with a sigh. Somewhere a man shouted, “¡Apagón!” Good. Let them think fried circuits. She grabbed the fire extinguisher from under the sink, hefted the weight. Short, red, sticky label half peeled. She moved low through the dining room, slipped between chairs. Her ankle throbbed with each heartbeat. In the foyer she saw the silhouette of rifle guy—same man who’d watched the curb earlier. He stepped inside, flashlight sweeping. The beam caught the mirror on the far wall, bounced blinding white into his own eyes. He cursed, turned. Marisol swung the extinguisher. The metal base caught him in the solar plexus. Air whooshed out; flashlight clattered. She didn’t wait. She flipped the pin, aimed the nozzle low, and pulled the trigger. White foam blasted across his boots, up his chest, into his open mouth. He gagged, blinded. She stepped in, used the extinguisher like a battering ram, driving him back against the half-open front door. Wood cracked. She brought her knee up into his groin, felt the soft give. He folded soundlessly. She stooped, scooped his rifle—an AR pistol with a cheap red dot. Magazine check by feel: ten, maybe twelve rounds left. Safety off. She slung it awkwardly across her back, muzzle down, the strap biting her collarbone. Windbreaker’s walkie crackled on her hip. Tigre’s voice, calm: “Status.” She pressed transmit with her thumb, held it close to her mouth. Let him hear her breathing, ragged and wet. “One down, one to go,” she said. She didn’t wait for reply. She killed the walkie, stuffed it into her waistband. From upstairs, a soft creak—board seven. Tigre was moving. She looked up the stairwell. Dark throat swallowing light. “House rules,” she muttered. “My turn.”
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 4 ("Garage Physics") is a tightly constructed action sequence that largely delivers on the model's stated intentions. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The prose style is well-calibrated to the plan's stated goals: sentences shorten during action, violence is clumsy and smells bad (gasoline, fertilizer, chemical stink), and there's a satisfying grittiness to the physical details. The garage-door trap works mechanically and feels earned rather than cinematic. The detail of "tiny brass screws pinged everywhere like hail" is precise and grounded. The ice-maker line jammed into an electrical outlet is the kind of improvised chaos that feels authentic to the character. The chapter follows its plan reasonably well. The plan mentioned "chain snaps, door drops on his forearm; he screams while she stomps the arm twice, maybe three times. She steals his pistol but forgets the safety is on; first shot deafens them all." The chapter diverges here—she doesn't steal the pistol in the same way, and the "first shot deafens them all" beat is entirely absent. Instead she takes a rifle from a different man. This is a notable deviation from the plan, and the plan's detail about the safety being on and the deafening shot was actually a strong, humanizing moment of incompetence that would have served the story well. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: Marisol's physicality and tactical thinking are consistent with her profile. The moral ledger stays appropriately open—she's efficient and brutal without being triumphant. The line "Garage physics. Lever, fulcrum, pain." is the closest thing to a one-liner, and the plan explicitly said to avoid heroic one-liners. It's not egregious, but it's a small violation of the stated aesthetic. DIALOGUE: Dialogue is sparse and functional. "Close enough to hear you beg" is a bit stock for a cartel man. Marisol's "One down, one to go" over the walkie is serviceable but not particularly distinctive. The plan called for dialogue that "overlaps, cuts off, repeats"—this chapter's dialogue is too clean and composed for that aesthetic. METAPHORS AND PROSE: Most metaphors land: "fingers twitching like busted antennae," "pulse jumped against it like a trapped moth," "dark throat swallowing light." These are in the jarhead register the plan called for. "Like a cat off a hot tin roof" is a cliché that doesn't fit the stated voice—it's the kind of ready-made phrase the plan should have avoided. "Hissed like a snake" is similarly lazy. The smart fridge "humming like a guilty witness" is a bit too cute, slightly purple. PACING AND TENSION: The chapter maintains good momentum. The escalation from garage to laundry room to foyer works spatially. The reader can track the geography. The tension of the kids upstairs while Marisol fights below is maintained without being belabored. WEAKNESSES: The chapter misses the plan's key beat about the pistol safety and the deafening shot—this was the most humanizing, anti-heroic moment planned for this chapter. The chapter ends with Marisol in a position of relative control, which slightly undercuts the stated goal of making readers "count bullets and heartbeats." The "House rules. My turn" closing line is dangerously close to a movie-poster quote, which the plan explicitly warned against. The foam extinguisher sequence is slightly too clean and competent for the stated aesthetic of clumsy violence. OVERALL: A solid, competent action chapter with good physical grounding and mostly appropriate prose register. It falls short of its own stated ambitions in a few key places—missing the planned incompetence beat, including a couple of near-one-liners, and not fully achieving the overlapping/cutting dialogue style. The violence is appropriately ugly but occasionally tips into choreographed competence rather than the planned clumsiness. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 13 Emotionally Engaging: 13 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 11 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 9 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 6 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 13 Characters Consistent with Profile: 14 Followed Chapter Plan: 11 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Marisol crouched behind the kitchen island, the AR pistol across her thighs, muzzle still warm. Outside, the sprinkler heads hissed awake—Theo’s doing. Water arced over the driveway in cheap rainbows that smelled of hose-water and chlorine. No gasoline in them; the kid had mis-read the valve labels. Still, the spray forced the SUV’s driver to duck back inside, headlights flicking on like startled eyes. The walkie—Tigre’s—clicked. “You always did like props, Maricruz. Lullabies, waterworks. Cute.” She didn’t answer. She was busy threading a new magazine—her last—into the rifle and listening to the house breathe. Somewhere upstairs a bedroom door eased shut with the soft finality of a coffin lid. Thirty feet away, the pantry doorknob rattled once, then stilled. Duct tape holding—she hoped. She keyed the radio, voice low. “Status?” Silence. Theo’s whisper came instead, through the cracked pantry door. “Sprinkler pressure maxed at forty psi. Still not flammable.” “Copy,” she said. “Keep counting.” “To what?” “Thirty.” She killed the channel. Tigre’s footsteps crossed the ceiling above her, measured, almost lazy. She pictured him: same steel-toe boots from the container yard, same notebook he used to fill with grocery lists. Eggs, bleach, flex-cuffs. She tasted blood—must have bitten her cheek again. Under the kitchen sink she found a half-gallon of cheap vodka, cap missing. She splashed it across the countertop, letting it run into the grout. Flammable, but she needed fire she could aim. Zippo snapped once, flame licking. She held it under the cupboard edge, let the wood begin to blacken, then snuffed it. Smoke curled, acrid. Enough to paint a target on the kitchen. Tigre’s voice drifted down the stairs, conversational. “Remember the song you sang? Rock-a-bye-baby, in the treetop. Same tune every goddamn night. My wife sang it to our boy before bed. You took that from me.” Marisol’s hand tightened on the rifle grip. She could still hear her own eighteen-year-old voice, cracked from dust, humming because it was either that or scream. She moved. Quiet steps to the base of the stairs, rifle leading. The carpet runner muffled her weight. At the fifth step she paused—board silent. Seventh groaned. She skipped it, kept climbing. Halfway up, the walkie crackled again. Theo, breathless: “Count thirty reached. Ellie saw something. Man in the pool—blue light on his watch. Every thirty seconds. One-Mississippi, two—” Marisol froze. Pool lights. That meant the back slider was open. She pivoted, rifle swinging. Too late. The laundry-room door at the top of the stairs burst outward and Tigre filled the frame, silhouette backlit by the washer’s glow. He held a tablet in one hand—live feed of the father in his safe-house chair, bruised, duct-taped. On screen, Captain Hensley’s mouth moved, no sound. “Look,” Tigre said softly. “He’s watching the kids tonight too. Just from a different room.” Marisol’s finger twitched on the trigger but she didn’t fire; Ellie’s small shape flickered behind Tigre in the hallway, barefoot, dragging the baby monitor like a leash. Tigre must’ve used her to find the hidden cameras. “Back downstairs, Ellie,” Marisol said. Ellie’s eyes were wide, fixed on the tablet. “Daddy’s tie is gray,” she whispered. Tigre smiled. “Gray tie day, right, pequeña?” Marisol lifted the rifle. “Let her go.” “Trade,” he said. “You for them. Walk outside, kneel, I let the brats run.” Theo’s voice crackled from the walkie, tinny: “The sprinkler timer resets in forty seconds.” Tigre raised an eyebrow. “Kid’s got timing.” Marisol’s mind raced. Forty seconds until the water shut off—then the driver would move again. She needed noise, cover, chaos. She flicked her gaze to the smoke curling up from the kitchen downstairs. “Clock’s ticking,” Tigre said. “Last time you chose wrong.” He stepped forward. Ellie flinched but didn’t move. On the tablet screen, Captain Hensley’s eyes widened—he’d spotted the rifle. Marisol saw his lips form one word: RUN. She fired. Not at Tigre—at the smoke detector above her head. The alarm shrieked, sudden and deafening. Sprinklers in the upstairs hall burst open, cold water streaming from cheap residential heads. Tigre jerked reflexively; tablet slipped, clattered. Marisol lunged, shoulder driving into his ribs, both of them crashing into the laundry room. The AR clattered away across wet tile. She landed on top, fists already moving—short, ugly punches to ribs and throat. Tigre bucked, stronger than she remembered. Elbow caught her temple, stars again. He rolled, pinning her, one hand going for the pistol on his hip. Ellie’s voice drifted through the siren: “Thirty seconds more.” Marisol drove her knee up, caught his gun arm at the wrist. The pistol barked once into the ceiling, showering plaster. She yanked the walkie from her belt, jammed it between them, and keyed transmit. “Now, Theo.” Downstairs, the sprinkler valve slammed shut with a hydraulic clunk. Water pressure surged—then reversed. Theo had cracked the backflow valve. A geyser of muddy water shot through the downstairs toilet, flooding the hall in seconds. Circuit breakers sparked. Lights died, leaving only the strobing red of the smoke alarm and the blue glow of Tigre’s fallen tablet. In the strobing dark, Marisol saw the pistol inches away. She grabbed for it. Tigre’s hand closed over hers at the same time. For a heartbeat they knelt in the laundry room, fingers interlaced around the gun, water soaking their knees. Tigre’s breath was warm against her ear. “Still humming that song?” She head-butted him, felt cartilage give. The gun twisted free, clattered across the tiles. She dove after it, hand closing on cold metal just as Ellie’s small voice counted down from five. At zero, the house went pitch black. Sprinkler pipes groaned like dying whales. Marisol backed out of the laundry room, pistol heavy in her grip, ankle throbbing, water dripping from taco pajamas. On the screen behind her, Captain Hensley’s face flickered—bruised, but alive. She whispered to the dark, “Gray tie day’s over.”
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong piece of pulp action writing that largely delivers on its promises. Let me examine it carefully across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The chapter maintains the tight, kinetic prose style promised in the writing plan. Sentences shorten during action sequences and the pacing is generally well-controlled. The detail work is strong: "taco pajamas," the vodka splashed across countertops, the smoke detector gambit, Theo's backflow valve trick—these feel earned and specific rather than generic action-movie beats. Character consistency is solid. Marisol's tactical thinking is shown through action rather than narration. Ellie's eidetic memory and pattern-fixation ("His watch beeps every thirty seconds") is used as a plot device in a way that feels organic to her character profile. Theo's hacker competence manifests practically. Tigre's psychological warfare—invoking the lullaby, the wife, the son—lands with genuine menace. The dialogue is mostly functional and avoids the movie-poster quality the plan warned against. "Gray tie day's over" at the end walks the line—it's almost a one-liner, but it's grounded in Ellie's established verbal tic, so it earns its place rather than feeling manufactured. The plan called for Tigre to come over the walkie "slow, familiar" and for Marisol to "almost puke." The chapter delivers the psychological weight of their history through the lullaby exchange, though the "almost puke" moment is somewhat muted—she bites her cheek, which is consistent with her character profile but perhaps undersells the visceral horror of the reunion. WEAKNESSES: The chapter deviates from its own plan in notable ways. The plan specified: "Theo hacks the sprinkler control; water hisses but only drenches the living-room rug, soaking the pistol. No Plan B." Instead, the chapter gives Theo a more elaborate and successful intervention (backflow valve, circuit breakers, flooding). This actually undermines the plan's stated goal of showing tech failing half the time and leaving Marisol without a Plan B. The chapter is more competent and tidy than the plan intended. The physical confrontation in the laundry room is somewhat generic—"short, ugly punches to ribs and throat," "stars again"—and doesn't fully deliver on the plan's promise that "violence is clumsy, loud, and smells bad." It's competent action writing but not particularly distinctive. Some metaphors are strained: "bedroom door eased shut with the soft finality of a coffin lid" is the kind of purple reach the plan explicitly warned against. "Sprinkler pipes groaned like dying whales" is similarly overwrought. "Headlights flicking on like startled eyes" is a cliché. The moral ledger the plan promised—Marisol hating that she likes violence—is largely absent from this chapter. We get tactical competence but not the psychological complexity that would elevate this above genre exercise. The Captain Hensley tablet reveal is a clever addition not in the plan, but it slightly clutters the chapter's focus. The "RUN" lip-read feels like exactly the kind of movie-poster moment the plan warned against. The chapter ends before the basement confrontation that the plan places in Chapter 7, which is appropriate—but the cliffhanger feels slightly manufactured rather than organic. Overall: a competent, engaging chapter that delivers on the genre promise but falls short of the more ambitious psychological and stylistic goals the plan set out. The action is readable and specific, the characters are recognizable, but the deeper texture—moral ambiguity, tech failure, visceral ugliness—is somewhat smoothed over. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 13 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 15 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 7 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 12 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 10 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The house was a throat now—dark, wet, and closing. Marisol slipped through it barefoot, pistol in one hand, hammer in the other. The hammer was a sixteen-ounce Estwing she’d taken off the pegboard after reloading the pistol with the last magazine—six rounds, no spare. The metal head was tacky with someone else’s blood and her own sweat. She kept the claw pointed forward because that’s what she’d used to open the dead man’s face downstairs and the memory felt useful. Water dripped from every ceiling seam. The sprinkler fuck-up had soaked drywall until it sagged like wet cardboard; each drop sounded like a slow clock. Somewhere a breaker popped, buzzed, gave up. The only steady light was the red exit sign over the back slider—battery emergency, one bulb flickering like a dying cigarette. She counted sounds: two men still breathing on their feet, one groaning under the garage door, Tigre upstairs with a busted nose. That left the driver somewhere outside, maybe already circling. Six heartbeats total. She felt hers loudest. Ellie and Theo were in the pantry with the second roll of duct tape Marisol had given them. “Count every drop,” she’d said. She didn’t tell them what drop meant. She moved through the dining room on all fours, knees squishing on the soaked rug. Clothes stuck to her skin: taco pajamas plastered to her thighs, the cotton so heavy with water she could wring it and fill a glass. She didn’t. She needed quiet. She needed to be small. Above her, Tigre’s boots creaked across the master bedroom. Slow, deliberate. Listening. She pictured him wiping blood from his upper lip with the back of his hand, smearing it like war paint. She pictured the pistol he’d picked up—hers now his. She pictured the folded photo of his kids she’d found in his wallet seventeen hours ago and felt nothing useful. Kitchen first. She needed something sharp that wasn’t a kitchen knife, something loud that wasn’t her last six rounds. The junk drawer caught her eye—she eased it open with two fingers. Inside: birthday candles, rubber bands, a half-bag of balloons. She pulled one balloon, red, still dusty from last Christmas. She needed the helium tank from Ellie’s birthday clown. It was in the garage with the broken guy. She’d have to go through him again. Garage door wouldn’t lift—chain still slack—so she slipped through the side door. The air hit her: gasoline, blood, mold. The wounded man was where she’d left him, zip-tied, half under the door. His breathing was wet and shallow. When he saw her he tried to yell through the gag; it came out a hiss like a busted radiator. She crouched. “Where’s the driver?” He shook his head, eyes rolling white. She held the pistol to the side of his knee—not the kneecap, the soft meat above it. “Where.” He jerked his chin toward the backyard. She nodded once, stood, and brought the hammer down hard on his temple. Not enough to kill, just enough to keep him folded and quiet. The helium tank stood in the corner next to a stack of soda flats. She grabbed the nozzle, twisted. Gas hissed out, cold against her palm. She stuffed the red balloon over the valve, let it swell until it tugged at her grip. When it was cantaloupe-sized she pinched the neck, tied it off with a bit of speaker wire from the workbench. She clipped the Zippo to the balloon with a paper clip, flame ready to kiss rubber when it met air. Back inside, she ghosted down the hallway. Somewhere a door hinge squealed; she froze, counted two seconds, moved again. She could hear the driver now—heavy footsteps on the patio stones, crunch-crunch-crunch, slow circles like he was pacing a deck of cards. He had a rifle slung, silhouette long against the pool light. She needed him looking up. She climbed the inside stairs again, skipping the seventh board. At the top, the hallway stretched left to the bedrooms, right to the attic hatch. A night-light shaped like a moon glowed faintly, leaking battery acid. She unscrewed it, pocketed the bulb—tiny glass shard for later. Tigre’s breathing came from the master bath. Door cracked, water still dripping from the ceiling like a slow leak. She crawled past, belly to carpet, and slipped into Theo’s room. Lego space debris under her knees, glow stars on the ceiling. The treehouse window overlooked the pool. Perfect. She cracked it open, cold night air rushing in. The driver stood below, rifle at low-ready, scanning. She dangled the helium balloon out by the knot, Zippo flame steady. Then she let it go. The red orb floated up, gentle as a lullaby. The driver’s flashlight found it immediately—bright target against black sky. He lifted the rifle. She shot the balloon. Pop—loud as a starter pistol. The Zippo fell, still lit. It hit the pool deck with a small whoomph of burning helium and alcohol. The driver flinched backward, rifle swinging upward. She had three seconds before he figured out the angle. She was already moving. Down the hall, past the bathroom. Tigre stepped out, pistol raised. She dropped low, swept his legs with the hammer. He fired past her ear into the ceiling. She rolled, came up inside his reach, and drove the hammer’s claw into his thigh. Meat parted; he roared. They grappled in the hallway, water pooling under their knees. His pistol skittered away. She felt his breath hot and sour on her face—coffee and copper. He punched her ribs, once, twice. She grabbed his wrist, twisted hard. Bones ground. He head-butted her forehead; stars again. She head-butted back. They crashed through Theo’s bedroom door, landed on Lego pieces that dug into her back like caltrops. She rolled off, coughing. Tigre crawled for his gun. She brought the hammer down on his wrist—heard the small, wet crunch of metacarpals. The pistol spun under the bed. He came up with a shard of broken Lego instead—razor-sharp—slashed at her cheek. She felt the sting, warmth running down to her jaw. She grabbed his hair—still the same short military crop—and slammed his face into the dresser. Once. Twice. On the third hit the drawer front cracked and his body went loose. She rolled off, panting. From downstairs came the sound of the pantry door creaking open and Theo’s voice—low, urgent. “Sprinklers off. Driver at pool. He’s looking up.” Marisol wiped blood from her eye, grabbed the hammer in both hands like a bat. She looked down at Tigre—nose mashed flat, breathing bubbly through broken cartilage. She whispered, “Playtime’s over.”
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 6 is a tightly constructed action sequence that largely delivers on the story's stated intentions. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The prose style is disciplined and consistent with the plan's mandate: short sentences during action, controlled rhythm throughout. "The house was a throat now—dark, wet, and closing" is a strong opener that earns its metaphor—it's visceral and functional, not decorative. The writing avoids the heroic one-liner trap almost entirely. "Playtime's over" at the end is the closest it comes, and it's delivered with enough exhaustion and context that it doesn't feel like a movie poster line. The physical specificity is excellent throughout. The Estwing hammer with its claw "pointed forward because that's what she'd used to open the dead man's face downstairs and the memory felt useful" is exactly the kind of grim, practical detail the plan called for. The taco pajamas plastered to her thighs, the Lego caltrops, the helium balloon improvisation—these feel earned and grounded. The balloon gambit is genuinely creative. It's a MacGyver solution that feels plausible within the story's logic, uses established props (Ellie's birthday clown supplies), and creates a real tactical moment. This is the chapter's strongest creative contribution. The violence is appropriately clumsy and ugly. The head-butt exchange, the metacarpal crunch, the Lego shard slash—none of it is glamorous. The plan specified "violence is clumsy, loud, and smells bad," and the chapter honors that. Marisol's character remains consistent with her profile: weight on the balls of her feet, tactical thinking, the grim practicality of knocking the garage man unconscious rather than killing him unnecessarily. WEAKNESSES: The chapter plan specified "Knives drawn, grunts and slipping blood. Marisol loses track—cuts her own palm, keeps squeezing. Cartel guy begs in Spanish; she answers in English because her mouth can't switch gears. Ends with him crawling toward the patio door, trailing loops of intestine." The actual chapter diverges significantly from this plan. There are no knives as the primary weapon, no begging in Spanish, no intestines. The chapter instead delivers a more elaborate tactical sequence involving the balloon and a confrontation with Tigre that was planned for Chapter 7. This is a meaningful deviation—the chapter essentially absorbs elements from multiple planned chapters and reshuffles the sequence. The moral ledger the plan promised—"Marisol hurts people because she's good at it and hates that she likes it"—is largely absent here. We get competence but not the self-disgust that makes Marisol interesting. The plan's psychological complexity gets sacrificed for action choreography. Dialogue is minimal, which is appropriate for the action context, but the brief exchange with the wounded garage man ("Where's the driver?" / head shake / knee threat / chin jerk) is functional rather than revealing. Theo's line from the pantry is efficient but thin. The opening metaphor "The house was a throat now—dark, wet, and closing" is good but the chapter occasionally reaches for similar effects that don't quite land: "leaking battery acid" describing a night-light's glow is a strange mixed image (night-lights don't leak battery acid visibly; it's trying to be evocative but is slightly incoherent). "Slow clock" for dripping water is generic. The counting of "six heartbeats total" is a nice touch but the math is slightly muddled—she counts "two men still breathing on their feet, one groaning under the garage door, Tigre upstairs with a busted nose" plus "the driver somewhere outside" plus herself. That's six, but the accounting is loose enough to be confusing on first read. The chapter ends with Tigre incapacitated but not dead, which contradicts the plan's Chapter 7 setup where Tigre holds Ellie in the basement. This reshuffling may create continuity problems. OVERALL: A competent, often impressive action chapter that prioritizes kinetic momentum over the psychological complexity the plan promised. The creative balloon gambit elevates it. The deviation from the chapter plan and the absence of the moral/emotional texture are real losses. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 11 Emotionally Engaging: 13 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 13 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 13 Characters Consistent with Profile: 14 Followed Chapter Plan: 7 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Basement stairs creaked once under her weight and then held their breath. Marisol went down sideways, hammer in one hand, Tigre’s pistol in the other. Water dripped from the ceiling onto the back of her neck—each drop a cold nail. The air smelled of mildew and chlorine, the way a pool smells when you leave the cover on too long. Tigre was already down there. She could feel him the way you feel a storm front: pressure change, skin prickling. The single bulb over the laundry tub flickered like a dying strobe. Shadows jumped across the washer and the folding table where Mrs. Hensley’s yoga pants still waited in a neat stack. Marisol stepped over them. Halfway down she saw Ellie—small, barefoot, thumb jammed in her mouth up to the second knuckle. Tigre had her by the hood of her unicorn pajamas, the pistol kissing the soft hollow under her ear. The baby monitor in his other hand showed Captain Hensley on a grainy feed: bruised, duct-taped to a motel chair, eyes wide. The audio was off, but the picture said plenty. “Close enough,” Tigre called up. Voice calm, almost gentle. “You taught me distance is just time with shoes on.” Marisol stopped on the second-to-last step. The pistol in her grip was slick with blood—hers or his, didn’t matter. Six rounds left if it was fully loaded. She couldn’t remember checking. “Trade,” Tigre said. “You for the girl. Walk down slow, kneel, I let her run.” Ellie’s eyes were fixed on Marisol’s face, counting freckles like always. No tears, just the steady suck of thumb. Marisol felt the hammer handle dig into her palm. She set it on the step behind her, metal head clinking. “Let her go first,” she said. Tigre smiled. Blood leaked from the corner of his mouth where she’d split his lip upstairs. “You always negotiate after you’ve already lost.” He pressed the muzzle harder. Ellie’s pajama collar twisted, fabric digging into her throat. The baby monitor crackled—static, then a voice she recognized: Captain Hensley, tinny and desperate. “Don’t you dare, Marisol. You hear me? Don’t you dare.” Tigre tilted the screen toward her. On it, Hensley strained against tape, chair legs scraping linoleum. Someone off-camera held a phone to his ear. Every thirty seconds the watch on that wrist flashed blue—same rhythm Ellie had counted earlier. Live feed. Real time. They were making him watch. Marisol took the last step down. Concrete cold under bare feet. She kept the pistol pointed low, safety off. “Let her go,” she said again. Tigre shook his head. “Knees first. Hands behind your head. Same position you put me in, Mogadishu.” She remembered the shipping container—air thick with diesel and piss, her voice humming because the MP3 had died. She remembered how after twelve hours he’d started crying to the beat of it. She hated that she remembered the tune. Water dripped on metal somewhere—ping, ping, ping—keeping time. Marisol lowered herself. One knee touched concrete. The other stayed cocked, ready. She set Tigre’s pistol on the floor, barrel facing him. Show of surrender. His eyes tracked it, greedy. Ellie’s thumb slipped out of her mouth. “Metal and oranges,” she whispered. Marisol nodded once. “I know, baby.” Tigre’s finger tightened on the trigger guard. “Hands behind your head, Maricruz.” She moved. Left hand shot to the pistol on the floor—not to grab it, to flick it. The slide racked loud in the small space. Tigre flinched sideways, muzzle pulling off Ellie’s neck for half a heartbeat. Enough. Marisol lunged. She drove her shoulder into Ellie, shoving the girl behind the washer. Tigre fired—round cracked past her ear, punched drywall. She came up inside his reach, forearm across his gun wrist, twisting. Bone grated. The pistol barked again, bullet whining off the concrete ceiling. They grappled, water underfoot turning the floor slick. She felt his breath, hot and sour, smelled the copper of his split lip. He kneed her ribs; something popped. She head-butted his already broken nose. Cartilage crunched. Blood sprayed warm across her cheek. They crashed into the folding table. Yoga pants avalanched to the floor. Tigre’s free hand scrabbled, found the hammer she’d set down earlier. He swung. She ducked. The claw buried in the drywall where her head had been. Marisol grabbed the baby monitor, smashed it against his temple. Plastic cracked, screen spider-webbed. Hensley’s face blinked out. Good—no more audience. Tigre roared, yanked the hammer free. She hooked an ankle behind his knee, drove him backward. They landed hard—her on top, water sloshing around them like a shallow tide. His pistol skittered under the dryer. His hands went for her throat. She felt thumbs dig in, pressure building black spots. She reached back, fingers scrabbling across the laundry tub’s edge. Found the hose—the one she’d spliced earlier—still spitting icy water. She shoved the nozzle into his open mouth and twisted the valve full. Water blasted. Tigre gagged, choked, hands loosening. She held the hose like a weapon, leaning her weight into it. His eyes bulged. He thrashed, boots drumming concrete. When the thrashing slowed she let go. Water trickled out of his mouth, mixed with blood. She rolled off, coughing. The hose slapped the floor, still gushing. Tigre rolled to his side, retching. She crawled to the dryer, reached underneath. Her fingers closed on cold metal—his pistol. She pulled it out, racked the slide. One round left in the chamber. She’d lost count of her own. She stood over him. Water pooled around his hips, pink with blood and bile. He looked up, eyes streaming. “Still humming?” she asked. He spat water. “Fuck you.” She pressed the muzzle to his temple. He didn’t flinch. Somewhere above them Ellie started counting—one-Mississippi, two-Mississippi—soft as breath. Marisol thought of the shipping container, of coconut shampoo and seventeen different traumas. She thought of the photo in his wallet—two kids on a plastic slide, sun behind them like halos. She pulled the trigger. The shot was sharp, final, swallowed by concrete and water. The body jerked once, then lay still. Water kept dripping, slow and steady, washing pink into the drain. Marisol dropped the pistol. It clattered like dropped silverware. Behind the washer, Ellie’s voice: “Thirty.” She turned. The little girl stood ankle-deep in water, unicorn pajamas soaked purple. She held out her hand. Marisol took it. It was warm and sticky—blood or ketchup, she couldn’t tell. “Let’s go find Theo,” she said. They climbed the stairs together, water dripping from every step like a trail they couldn’t stop leaving behind.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong chapter that largely delivers on the plan's promises. Let me work through it carefully. **Strengths:** The chapter opens with effective sensory grounding—the creaking stairs, the cold drip on the neck, the mildew-and-chlorine smell. These details are specific without being overwrought. The plan called for a basement confrontation where Tigre holds Ellie, negotiation stutters, Ellie bites his wrist, and Marisol takes a bullet through the thigh before burying pruning shears in his neck. The actual chapter diverges significantly from this plan: there's no wrist bite, no bullet through the thigh, no pruning shears. Instead, the resolution involves a hose shoved in Tigre's mouth and then a point-blank execution. This is a notable deviation from the plan, though the substitution is reasonably effective. The baby monitor showing Captain Hensley is a smart addition—it raises stakes and adds a witness dimension. The "metal and oranges" callback from Ellie's character profile is well-deployed and earns its emotional weight. Ellie counting "one-Mississippi" at the end is genuinely affecting. The execution scene—Marisol pressing the muzzle to Tigre's temple after he's already incapacitated—is morally complex in the right way. The plan called for the moral ledger to stay open, and this delivers: she executes a helpless man. The brief thought about the photo of his kids and "seventeen different traumas" is the right kind of moral weight without being preachy. **Weaknesses:** The hose-in-the-mouth resolution is somewhat implausible and feels like a tonal mismatch—it reads almost cartoonish compared to the gritty register the story has established. The plan's pruning shears were more viscerally grounded. "You always negotiate after you've already lost" is a line that sounds like it wants to be quoted on a movie poster—exactly what the writing plan said to avoid. Similarly, "distance is just time with shoes on" is the kind of pseudo-profound line the plan explicitly warned against. The fight choreography is competent but occasionally loses spatial coherence. The hose that was "spliced earlier" is introduced without prior establishment in this chapter, which creates a minor continuity issue (though it may have been set up in a previous chapter). The dialogue is mostly functional but occasionally slips into genre convention. "Fuck you" as Tigre's last words before execution is deliberately flat, which works. But some of Tigre's lines feel like villain-speak. The ending—"water dripping from every step like a trail they couldn't stop leaving behind"—is the chapter's most overtly poetic moment. It's not terrible, but it's the kind of line that strains slightly for effect. The chapter is coherent and maintains the locked-tight third-person perspective well. Marisol's interiority is handled with restraint—we get the shipping container memory without over-explanation. The physical details (yoga pants avalanching, the hammer claw in drywall) are specific and grounded. Character consistency is strong: Ellie's counting, her "metal and oranges" phrase, Tigre's calm menace, Marisol's tactical thinking.
