Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Character 1 – Gethin Rhys Background Born in Splott, Cardiff, to a dinner-lady mother and a taxi-driver father who both claimed Swansea ancestry when drunk. Left Wales at fifteen for a London stage school; spent the next two decades sanding his accent into something “mid-Atlantic” that still slips when he’s tired or furious. Made his name playing louche aristocrats and damaged spies, but every role eats a bit of the original accent and he’s quietly terrified there’s nothing left underneath. Flies home for Mam’s birthdays, then escapes before the real conversation starts. Has a flat in Primrose Hill he never quite furnishes and a PA who schedules his panic attacks between junkets. Personality & Idiosyncrasies • Cannot leave a room without trying to make everyone like him; will compliment a stranger’s shoes even if they’re Crocs. • Hoards receipts because he’s convinced the tax man thinks actors are liars. • Still uses the same cheap supermarket shaving foam that smells of fake lemons—he’s allergic to anything fancier. • Taps a two-beat rhythm on his thigh when lying (learned it from a dialect coach). • Has a running bet with himself: if he can make a journalist laugh in the first thirty seconds, the interview will be kind. Loses often. Physicality Five-ten, but stands like he’s waiting to be measured again. Dark hair that never quite stays where the stylist wants; one eyebrow sits higher when he’s listening, giving him a permanent half-skeptical air. Moves in quick, economical bursts—actor training—but fidgets with the cuffs of any jumper until the wrists stretch. When caught off-guard his shoulders fold inward, suddenly teenage. Motives, Allegiances, Relationships Wants to be liked, but more urgently wants to be *recognised* as the kid who once knew every bus stop between Splott and Grangetown. Loyal to his mother, wary of his father’s silence, indebted to an agent who still calls him “boyo” in meetings. No steady partner—two amiable exes who both said variations of “you perform love better than you do it.” Flavour Voice Dialogue: “Look, I’m not being modest—I’m being accurate. The accent’s a rental, same as the tux.” Internal: *If she laughs at the joke, I can breathe. If she doesn’t, I’ll try the next one. And the next. Christ, I’m exhausting.* # Character 2 – Morgan Evans Background Raised in Aberaeron in the flat above her parents’ chippy; learned to read by sounding out the chip-shop menu to tourists. Got a first at Aberystwyth, then a PhD bursary in Cardiff that stalled when her mother’s lungs packed up. Came home “for six months” six years ago, took over The Crooked Shelf because it was going under and the bank manager knew her dad. Still owes him. Hasn’t finished a novel in four years but can quote the shipping forecast from memory. Personality & Idiosyncrasies • Shelves books by emotional temperature rather than alphabet; crime fiction lives next to cookbooks because “both involve knives.” • Talks to the stock when she thinks no one’s listening—apologises to poetry for bending the corners. • Keeps a chipped mug labelled “World’s Okayest Daughter” that her brother gave her the week their mother died. • Cannot drive past a charity shop without stopping for “just one box.” • Has a precise, almost violent way of tearing sellotape with her teeth. Physicality Five-six, broad-shouldered, hair the colour of wet sand and usually twisted up with whatever pen was nearest. Ink on her cuticles, flour on her elbows from helping next door. Walks like someone perpetually carrying too many bags; when she stands still her feet point out at ten-to-two, leftover ballet posture from childhood lessons she quit after one term. Motives, Allegiances, Relationships Runs the shop because it’s the last thing her mother loved that still needs her. Pays the electricity before her own invoices. Loyal to the town’s rhythm—early closing on Wednesdays, Saturday market gossip—but quietly allergic to anyone who treats her as local colour. Single by exhaustion rather than choice; last date ended when he asked if the sea view ever got boring. Flavour Voice Dialogue: “If you want the loo it’s up the stairs, first door on the left. Mind the step—eighteenth-century floors and twentieth-century plumbing don’t speak the same language.” Internal: *He’s trying out accents like shirts in a sale. I should charge by the minute.*

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The rain had started as drizzle, the kind Aberaeron wore like cheap perfume, but by the time Gethin reached the harbour it had turned spiteful—needles of water driving sideways off the slate-grey sea. The paparazzi had been polite at first, forming a loose semi-circle outside the café where he’d eaten a grimly virtuous bowl of cawl. Then someone shouted a question about his ex-co-star’s wedding, another flash popped, and his public smile cracked. He ducked left, past the lifeboat station, past the chip shop that still smelled of vinegar at eleven in the morning, and saw the narrow, crooked sign: BOOKS. The door was already open, as if the shop had been waiting. Inside, the air was warmer and smelled of damp wool and something sharper—old paper, maybe fried onions from next door. Books were stacked like barricades. Somewhere above him, a woman’s voice muttered: “Come *on*, you bastard,” followed by the soft rip of tape. Gethin looked up. Morgan Evans was halfway up a wooden ladder, wrestling a box of remainders onto a high shelf. She wore a navy jumper gone bobbly at the elbows, sleeves shoved to the shoulder. A pen skewered her hair like a misplaced chopstick. Without glancing down she said, “Shut the door—heat’s dearer than books.” He obeyed, then waited for recognition to hit. It didn’t. The woman descended two rungs, still absorbed in whatever rebellion the box was staging. Gethin cleared his throat, the actor’s reflex—audible, charming, a little needy. “I’m not buying,” he said, because it sounded rakish. “Merely sheltering.” Now she looked. Brown eyes, steady, slightly narrowed, as if calculating overdue fines. “Sheltering from what, rain or consequences?” “Both, if I’m honest.” He gave the half-smile that magazine covers loved. “Rain’s easier to outrun.” Morgan stepped off the ladder, boots landing soft on the threadbare rug. She took in the damp hair plastered to his forehead, the hoodie worth more than most people’s rent, the paparazzi silhouette still hovering outside the steamed glass. Her expression didn’t shift; she simply walked to the counter and began fitting a new till roll with the violent efficiency of someone re-loading a stapler. “You’re that bloke,” she said, not a question. He waited for the usual inflection—delight, accusation, the small star-struck gasp. She added nothing. The roll clicked into place. Gethin felt the first prickle of sweat under his collar. “Gethin Rhys,” he supplied, just in case she’d mistaken him for the dentist off *Casualty*. “I know your name. Doesn’t mean I want it shouted in here.” She nodded toward the door, where a flash went off like a small explosion. “They’ll follow you in if you stand there posing.” He stepped deeper into the shop, past towers of paperbacks that leaned like drunks. The floorboards creaked a warning. Somewhere overhead a slow drip counted time. “Is there a back exit?” he asked. “Through the coal hole, if you fancy Victorian ventilation.” She peeled tape with her teeth, tore it clean. “Or you could wait. They get bored quicker than toddlers.” The bell above the door—hung from a frayed ribbon—chose that moment to tinkle as a tourist pushed in, dripping. Morgan moved to intercept. “We’re closing for lunch.” “It’s eleven-thirty,” the tourist protested. “Early lunch,” Morgan said, and steered the woman back onto the pavement. Click of the lock, turn of the sign from OPEN to BACK IN FIVE—though she didn’t set a clock. Gethin watched her, fascinated and off-balance. Usually people angled for selfies by now. He picked up the nearest book—*Coastal Walks of Ceredigion*—and pretended to read. The silence stretched until it hummed. “So,” he tried, “what time do you reopen?” “When the kettle boils.” She leaned on the counter, arms folded. No invitation in it. “You planning to browse or just bleed on my carpet?” He looked down; rainwater had pooled off his hoodie onto the rug, forming a dark, sheep-shaped stain. “Sorry. Force of habit—making an entrance.” “Try an exit next time.” But her mouth twitched, the ghost of a grin that hadn’t decided whether to stay. She reached under the counter, produced a tea-towel that might once have been white, and lobbed it at him. He caught it one-handed, reflexes still sharp from fight scenes he never performed himself. Gethin dabbed ineffectually at the rug. “I could pay for dry-cleaning,” he offered. “It’s a rug, not a prom dress.” She took the towel back, folded it with surprising neatness. “Anyway, you’re stuck till the vultures migrate. May as well make yourself useful.” She thrust a barcode scanner into his hand. It was warm from her grip, plastic chipped at the corner. Gethin stared at it. “Inventory,” she said. “Start with the poetry. Lowest foot traffic.” “Do I get employee discount?” “You get shelter.” She was already turning away, climbing the ladder again, the treads groaning under her boots. Outside, the rain redoubled, drumming on the corrugated roof like an impatient audience. Gethin looked at the scanner, at the leaning shelves, at the back of her jumper riding up just enough to show a sliver of skin above her belt. He pressed the trigger
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong opening chapter that demonstrates considerable craft. Let me assess it systematically. **Characterization and Voice** Gethin is rendered with precision: the reflexive charm, the half-smile "that magazine covers loved," the prickle of sweat when recognition doesn't come. The detail about him catching the tea-towel "one-handed, reflexes still sharp from fight scenes he never performed himself" is economical and revealing. Morgan is equally well-drawn—her violence with the sellotape, her flat refusal to perform delight, the "ghost of a grin that hadn't decided whether to stay." Both characters are consistent with their profiles. Morgan's line "Sheltering from what, rain or consequences?" establishes her intelligence and dry wit immediately. Gethin's "I'm not being modest—I'm being accurate" energy is present throughout without being stated. **Dialogue** The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. It crackles with asymmetry—Gethin performing, Morgan declining to be an audience. "You're that bloke" as a non-question is perfect. "It's a rug, not a prom dress" lands cleanly. The exchanges feel genuinely unscripted, with the characters talking past each other in productive ways. The dialogue does the heavy lifting without announcing itself. **Prose Quality** The opening description—"rain Aberaeron wore like cheap perfume"—is a strong image that earns its metaphorical weight. "Books were stacked like barricades" is functional and apt. "The silence stretched until it hummed" is slightly overworked but not egregious. The chip-shop vinegar detail, the frayed ribbon bell, the sheep-shaped water stain—these are the kind of specific, unshowy details the plan promised and the chapter delivers. The ending on "He pressed the trigger" is a clean, confident cut. **Weaknesses** The phrase "like drunks" for leaning paperbacks is a mild cliché. "Drumming on the corrugated roof like an impatient audience" is a touch too neat—the theatrical metaphor is slightly on-the-nose given the character. The chapter follows its plan faithfully but perhaps too faithfully; the beats land almost exactly as described, which means there are few genuine surprises within the chapter itself. The "sliver of skin above her belt" moment at the end is the one instance where the prose edges toward the predictable romantic-tension playbook. **Adherence to Plan and Prompt** The chapter follows its plan closely: rain, ladder, scanner, locked door, the tourist turned away. The prompt's requirements—charming rogue, immune bookstore owner, unspoken chemistry, primarily dialogue—are all met. The Welsh setting is rendered with specificity (Aberaeron, cawl, the harbour, Welsh particles in the plan though not yet in the text). The chapter ends at the right moment. **Overall Assessment** This is accomplished, controlled writing. It avoids purple prose, earns its lightness, and establishes two distinct, compelling voices. The chemistry is present without being announced. Minor quibbles about a few slightly predictable images don't significantly diminish what is a very strong opening chapter. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Gethin had never known poetry to be so vindictive. The poetry section—three shelves wedged between “Local Interest” and “True Crime”—was narrow enough that every spine he touched seemed to bite back. He aimed the scanner at a tatty Sylvia Plath. *Beep.* The machine gave a sickly whine, red light fluttering. *Beep-fail.* He turned the book over, found the barcode had been peeled off like a scab. “Your scanner’s broken,” he called. “It’s the books that are broken,” Morgan answered from somewhere behind Crime. “People nick the stickers for scrapbooking. Scan the shelf label instead.” He squinted at the handwritten tag: POE-TRY. Someone had added a biro arrow and the word *miserable*. Gethin pressed the trigger. *Beep.* A small green victory. He felt absurdly pleased. Footsteps approached; Morgan appeared with a stack of Maeve Binchys. Without asking, she dumped half into his arms. “These go top right. You’re tall—don’t make me climb again.” He followed her up the aisle, breathing in second-hand paper and the faint chip-fat smell that drifted through the wall from next door. When she stretched to shelve, her sleeve brushed his cheek—wool and something peppery, like old soap. Gethin fought the urge to lean in. He hadn’t been this close to a woman who hadn’t Googled him first in years. “So,” he said, slotting a Binchy between *Quilt as You Go* and *Shellfish of Cardigan Bay*, “what’s the record for most books shelved without a customer buying one?” “Yesterday,” she said. “Eleven. And that included the woman who mistook the loo for the maritime history section.” He laughed—too loud, he knew, the stage laugh tuned for the back row. Morgan glanced sideways, eyebrow raised. He dialled it down. “Honestly,” she said, “if you’re going to loiter, you could dust.” She pulled a yellow microfibre cloth from her pocket and flicked it at him. It smelled faintly of vinegar. Gethin wiped the spine of a battered Catherine Cookson. “I played a 19th-century footman once. Terrible wig.” “Was it harder than scanning barcodes?” “Marginally more dancing.” He glanced at her profile. “You really don’t care, do you?” “About footmen? Not especially.” “About—” He gestured vaguely at himself, the implication hanging like wet laundry. Morgan paused, book mid-air. “Should I ask for an autograph? Charge entry? Tell you you were robbed at the BAFTAs?” He winced. “That bad?” “You’re breathing my air without buying anything. That’s the crime I’m prosecuting.” She softened—barely. “Besides, Aberaeron’s had stranger. Last year a Bond villain bought a caravan here. Kept trying to pay for milk with euros.” Before he could answer, a thump rattled the door. A man in a waxed jacket cupped his hands to the glass. Camera strap round his neck like a leash. “Paps?” Morgan asked, though she’d already guessed. “Rear guard.” Gethin ducked instinctively. “If he sees me, we’ll be swamped.” She eyed the door, then him. “Cupboard under the poetry stairs. Go.” He folded himself among hoover bags and a stack of damp *National Geographic*. The cupboard smelled of mouse traps and forgotten coffee. Through the slats he watched Morgan unbolt the door a crack. “Shop’s shut,” she said. “Just a quick question, love.” The man’s voice oozed charm. “Seen—” “No.” She shut the door. Locked it. Turned the sign with a definitive flick. Gethin emerged, knees cracking. “You enjoy that?” “I enjoy no being shorthand for gossip.” She nudged the hoover bags back with her foot. “They’ll give up when the chippy opens.” He leaned on the doorframe, suddenly conscious of how small the shop felt with the two of them in it. “I could pay you for the inconvenience.” “Could you pay me in silence?” She said it lightly, but the words landed heavy. He pressed his lips together like a chastised schoolboy. Silence stretched, thick as dust motes. Somewhere behind the counter, a kettle began its prehistoric wheeze. “Tea?” she asked, grudging. He followed the sound. The kettle was orange
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is Chapter 2 of a planned eight-chapter story, and it largely delivers on the promise of the writing plan and character profiles. Let me assess it carefully across the relevant dimensions. STRENGTHS: The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. Lines like "Could you pay me in silence?" land with genuine wit and character specificity. Morgan's voice is consistently dry, economical, and slightly combative without tipping into caricature. Gethin's stage laugh "tuned for the back row" that he then consciously dials down is an excellent piece of showing-not-telling that reveals his self-awareness about his own performance instincts. The exchange about the BAFTAs is sharp and efficient. The physical comedy of Gethin folding himself into a cupboard among hoover bags and damp National Geographics is well-executed and tonally appropriate—it deflates his celebrity without being cruel. Morgan's curt dismissal of the paparazzo ("No.") followed by her explanation ("I enjoy no being shorthand for gossip") is a strong character moment that reveals her values without announcing them. The sensory details are mostly earned: the chip-fat smell through the wall, the vinegar-scented cloth, the mouse-trap-and-forgotten-coffee cupboard. These feel lived-in rather than decorative. The biro arrow adding "miserable" to the POE-TRY shelf label is a lovely small touch. The chemistry between the characters is handled with appropriate restraint. The moment where Morgan's sleeve brushes his cheek and he "fought the urge to lean in" is effective precisely because it's brief and immediately undercut by his self-aware observation about women who haven't Googled him. WEAKNESSES: The chapter ends abruptly mid-sentence: "The kettle was orange" — this is clearly a truncation error, not an artistic choice. It's a significant flaw that leaves the chapter feeling incomplete. This is the most notable technical failure. Some metaphors are slightly strained. "Camera strap round his neck like a leash" is competent but not particularly fresh. "Silence stretched, thick as dust motes" is a bit of a cliché construction. "Words landed heavy" is a mixed metaphor (words landing like wet laundry was already used earlier in the same paragraph). The plan called for Gethin to misidentify a Catherine Cookson as Jane Austen and "get a snort for it" — this is partially delivered (he mentions the Cookson) but the misidentification joke is dropped. The plan also mentioned the scanner dying and an argument over batteries/phones, and Gethin's manager ringing — none of these appear. The chapter follows the spirit of the plan but skips several of its specific beats. The observation "He hadn't been this close to a woman who hadn't Googled him first in years" is slightly on-the-nose in explaining his psychology, though it's not egregiously so. The Bond villain anecdote from Morgan feels slightly inserted — it's charming but doesn't quite connect to anything. OVERALL: This is confident, well-crafted prose with strong dialogue and good character consistency. The truncation is a real problem, and a few metaphors don't fully land, but the chapter succeeds at its core task of building chemistry through friction. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 12 Coherent: 13 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 11 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The kettle finished its death-rattle and Morgan poured water over two bags of supermarket own-brand tea. Steam fogged her glasses; she took them off, wiped them on her sleeve, and handed Gethin a chipped mug that read I’M A MUG in faded Comic Sans. He turned it in his hands, looking for the joke. “Gift from my brother,” she said. “He finds himself hilarious.” Gethin took a sip. It tasted of cardboard and tannin, but it was hot. They stood at the counter, elbows almost touching, the silence now companionable instead of weaponised. Rain still lashed the windows in irregular bursts, like someone flicking peas at the glass. “So,” he said, nodding toward the shelves, “fairy-tales. You were going to tell me how badly I butchered the title.” Morgan snorted. “Mabinogi, not ‘mabin-oh-gee’. And it’s not one story, it’s four bleeding branches.” She slid off the stool, disappeared between aisles, and returned with a small, cloth-bound book the colour of dried blood. The gold lettering on the spine had flaked away in places. She laid it on the counter between them like evidence. Gethin opened it carefully. Inside the front cover someone had written, in blunt pencil, “If lost, return to Morgan E. 2001.” The handwriting looked younger, rounder. “That was my rebellion,” she said. “Everyone else got tattoos. I got footnotes.” He ran a thumb over the page. The paper felt soft, almost damp, as if it had absorbed twenty-odd years of coastal air. “I nicked this copy from the school library,” she went on. “Told Mrs Llewellyn I’d lost it. Cost me two quid fine and eternal guilt.” “You still feel guilty over two quid?” “Over Mrs Llewellyn. She believed me.” Morgan tapped the page. “See this bit? ‘Pryderi, son of Pwyll, lord of Dyfed…’ You called him ‘Pry-derry’ last night.” He winced. “I was twelve. And American casting directors kept saying ‘make it easier for us’.” “Easier isn’t the same as right.” She said it without heat, more like she was reminding herself. Gethin closed the book and set it down. “Say it properly, then.” “What, now?” “Humiliate me. Educational purposes.” Morgan raised an eyebrow, then leaned across him and spoke the line slow and sure, the Welsh rolling like surf over round stones. The sound made the small shop feel suddenly bigger, older. When she finished, she waited. He tried to echo her, tripped over the double-l, and ended up somewhere between a sneeze and a hiccup. She laughed—properly this time, the kind that showed a chipped incisor and made her eyes disappear. He laughed too, surprised at how good it felt to fail at something insignificant. “Worse than I remembered,” he admitted. “Keep practicing. You’ve got the vowels of a tourist with head-cold.” She took the book back, hugged it to her chest a moment before shelving it behind the till. “Why’d you stop speaking it?” He shrugged. “Boarding school. First week they made me read aloud in assembly. I said ‘nos da’ like ‘nose dah’. Whole hall laughed. After that I just… filed it away.” Morgan’s expression flickered—something between sympathy and irritation. “That’s one way to treat a language.” “Not proud of it.” He turned the mug again, as if the words might be printed inside. “I kept the stories, though. In my head. Thought that counted.” “Head’s a start. Tongue’s better.” She refilled both mugs without asking. The second cup tasted slightly less like cardboard. They drifted back to the poetry shelves, shoulder to shoulder. Morgan pulled down a slim volume—R. S. Thomas—and handed it to him. “Read the last stanza. Out loud. No acting.” He cleared his throat, suddenly nervous. The poem was short, bleak, beautiful. His voice wavered on the final line, caught on the word “hiraeth” like a snag in cloth. When he finished, the shop felt quieter, as if the rain had paused to listen. Morgan didn’t speak for a long moment. Then she reached across, took the book, and slid it back exactly where it had been. “Not bad,” she said. “Still too clean, but not bad.” He found himself watching the way she moved—economical, certain, the way she tucked a stray strand of hair behind her ear with the same gesture she used to mark pages. There was ink on the side of her index finger, a small blue bruise of it, and he had to stop himself asking how it got there. Instead he asked, “You ever miss the PhD?” She exhaled through her nose. “Miss having opinions nobody wanted to pay for? Sometimes.” She gestured at the shop. “This lot argues back less.” “And pays worse, I bet.” “Pays in gossip and the occasional homemade bara brith.” She glanced at him. “You ever miss being nobody?” The question landed harder than he expected. He opened his mouth, closed it, then said, “I miss being able to walk into a chippy without a plan.” Morgan nodded like she understood, though she couldn’t possibly. She picked up the Welsh fairy-tale book again, hesitated, then pressed it into his hands. “Borrow it. Bring it back with the accent fixed.” He stared at the cover. “What if I mess it up worse?” “Then you’ll owe me another two quid and eternal guilt.” Her smile was small but real. Outside, a gull screamed. Inside, the kettle clicked off. They stood there, the book between them, the rain easing from assault to apology. Gethin realised he hadn’t checked his phone in an hour; the thought felt like discovering he’d forgotten a limb. Morgan broke the spell first. “Tea’s gone cold. And I’ve got invoices.” He nodded, but didn’t move. The book felt heavier than it should, as if all the stories inside had suddenly noticed they were being watched.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong piece of character-driven romantic fiction that largely succeeds at what it sets out to do. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. DIALOGUE AND VOICE: The dialogue is genuinely one of the chapter's strongest elements. Both characters speak in distinct, believable voices. Morgan's lines have the quality of someone who has thought carefully about language and uses it precisely: "Easier isn't the same as right," "Head's a start. Tongue's better," "Still too clean, but not bad." These feel earned rather than constructed. Gethin's self-deprecating charm comes through naturally: "Humiliate me. Educational purposes." The exchange about the Welsh pronunciation is particularly well-handled—it does real character work while also being genuinely funny. The dialogue misfire the plan promised ("half-finished sentences, overlapping talk, accidental insults") is somewhat present but less pronounced than planned; the conversation is actually quite smooth and witty, which slightly undercuts the "muddy feelings" intention. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: Both characters are well-rendered against their profiles. Gethin's ego-fragility surfaces in the assembly anecdote—the wound is real and specific. His charm offensive is present but genuinely derailed by Morgan's precision. Morgan's emotional temperature shelving, her guilt over Mrs Llewellyn, her ink-stained finger, her way of moving—all feel consistent with the profile. The moment where she hugs the book to her chest before shelving it is a lovely piece of showing. His watching her hand and stopping himself from asking about the ink is exactly the right kind of restrained attraction. METAPHOR AND PROSE QUALITY: Most of the metaphors land. "Rain still lashed the windows in irregular bursts, like someone flicking peas at the glass" is fresh and specific. "The Welsh rolling like surf over round stones" is perhaps slightly expected but works in context. "The book felt heavier than it should, as if all the stories inside had suddenly noticed they were being watched" is the chapter's most self-conscious metaphor—it's a bit too neat, a bit too announced, and represents the kind of symbolism the plan explicitly said to avoid ("avoid symbolism that announces itself"). The final image of the book being "watched" is the weakest moment in an otherwise controlled piece. FOLLOWING THE PLAN: The plan for Chapter 3 called for: her finding him staring at a battered Welsh fairy-tale book he thinks he remembers, him getting the title wrong, Morgan correcting him in Welsh, them ending up sitting on the floor between shelves with knees almost touching, and both standing up too fast. The chapter delivers the book, the correction, the Welsh, and the chemistry—but notably omits the floor-sitting moment, which was the plan's central physical beat of near-intimacy. Instead the intimacy is handled through the R.S. Thomas reading and the book-lending. This is a meaningful deviation; the floor scene would have been more physically grounded and less literary-cute. The chapter substitutes one kind of intimacy (shared reading) for another (shared floor space), which works but loses some of the awkward physicality the plan promised. EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT: The chapter is genuinely moving in places. The assembly anecdote—"I said 'nos da' like 'nose dah'. Whole hall laughed"—is the emotional core and it lands cleanly. The question "You ever miss being nobody?" and his answer about the chippy is excellent. These feel like real revelations rather than performed ones. PURPLE PROSE / FORCED POETRY: Mostly avoided. The prose is clean and purposeful. The one exception is the final paragraph's "as if all the stories inside had suddenly noticed they were being watched"—this is the kind of self-announcing symbolism the plan warned against, and it slightly deflates an otherwise well-controlled ending. PLOT: There isn't much plot in the traditional sense, but this is appropriate for the chapter's function. It's a scene of deepening connection, and it does that job well. The bara brith mention, the R.S. Thomas reading, the book-lending—these are small, specific, Welsh-grounded details that feel lived-in rather than researched. OVERALL: A confident, well-crafted chapter that delivers on most of its promises. The dialogue is its strongest asset. The main weaknesses are: the floor-sitting scene from the plan is absent (replaced by something more literary but less physically awkward), and the final metaphor is slightly too neat. The Welsh cultural texture is handled with care and specificity. The chemistry is present and believable without being overwrought. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The rain stopped being charming sometime after midnight. It turned vindictive, hurling itself against the skylight like gravel tipped from a lorry. Morgan heard the first drip at the far end of the top-floor flat—plink—then a second, then a steady tap-tap-tap that said the roof had finally given up. She padded barefoot down the narrow stairs, Gethin a reluctant shadow behind her. He’d insisted on “helping,” which really meant hovering with the torch while she swore at the ceiling. The torch was an ancient rubber thing that weighed about the same as a brick and gave off a sickly yellow beam. “Left a bit,” she muttered, shoving the ladder across the landing. “No, my left. Christ, have you ever held anything heavier than a script?” “I once carried an entire awards ceremony,” he said, steadying the ladder. “Very heavy ego in that room.” She climbed, pyjama bottoms catching on a rung. The drip hit her cheek, cold as melted coins. She poked the plaster experimentally; a coin-sized bulge wept brown water. “Great. That’s right over my bed.” Gethin passed up a saucepan. She balanced it on the beam. The drip became a metallic ping every three seconds—doomed metronome. They hauled buckets, saucepans, even an old roasting tin. Each new vessel meant another trip down the stairs, past teetering stacks of unsold hardbacks, through the shop that smelled now of wet wool and rising panic. By the third trip Gethin had rolled his hoodie sleeves, revealing a small scar on his wrist—white, neat, like someone had tried to erase him. “How’d you get that?” Morgan asked, nodding. “Stunt gone wrong. Pretended to fall off a balcony, actually fell off a balcony.” He gave the grin that usually got him out of follow-up questions. She only hmmed, handed him another bucket. Back in the flat, the ceiling had developed three new leaks. Morgan stood in the middle of her bedroom—really just a mattress on pallets—watching water drip onto her duvet like a slow-motion stoning. “Bollocks.” She stripped the bed with violent efficiency, sheets landing in a damp heap. Gethin hovered, torch beam wobbling. “I could—” “Take the end.” Together they dragged the mattress to the driest corner, a space barely big enough for a cat. Morgan’s pillow was already soaked; she lobbed it toward the landing without ceremony. It hit the wall with a sodden thud. The kettle downstairs had tripped the fuse earlier. They ended up back in the shop, wrapped in mismatched blankets from the half-price box, drinking tea made with water boiled in a saucepan over the camping stove Morgan kept for power cuts. The stove hissed, blue flame reflecting in puddles on the lino. Gethin wrapped his fingers round the mug. “I once did a film where the set flooded. Whole soundstage ankle-deep. Producer called it ‘authentic atmosphere.’” “Did they pay you extra for trench foot?” “Just hazard rates. And a standing ovation at wrap party.” He took a sip, winced. “This tastes like burnt rubber.” “That’ll be the saucepan. Previous owner made nettle soup in it.” Morgan pulled her blanket tighter. She’d changed into an oversized rugby shirt advertising a pub she’d never been to. The hem reached her knees; her feet, in holey socks, poked out like guilty secrets. The rain had settled into a steady roar now, like static on an old radio. Somewhere in Crime, water dripped into a bucket with the rhythm of a slow heartbeat. Morgan listened, counting. Gethin watched her profile—small nose, freckle on the bridge, eyelashes still spiky from earlier leaks. “My mum always said Welsh roofs were built to let the weather in,” she said suddenly. “Keeps the houses honest.” “Mine said buy a flat in London and never look back.” He tried for lightness; it came out flat. Morgan studied him. “You ever go back?” “To the flat or to Cardiff?” “Either.” He shrugged, blanket slipping off one shoulder. “Cardiff feels like a play I walked out of at intermission. Everyone else knows the ending.” She nodded, as if that made perfect sense. The camping stove flared; shadows jumped across stacks of books. Gethin noticed a paperback wedged under one table leg, spine cracked to keep the whole thing level. He pointed. “That’s criminal.” “Desperate times. It’s *Fifty Shades*. It’ll cope.” He laughed—proper, unguarded. The sound startled them both. Morgan found herself smiling before she could stop it. Minutes passed. The rain softened, then returned with renewed fury, like it had gone to fetch reinforcements. Gethin’s eyelids sagged; he jerked awake when his head touched the edge of a shelf. “Bed’s off limits,” Morgan said. “But sofa’s negotiable.” She jerked her chin toward the sagging two-seater under the window, currently hosting a pile of charity-shop jigsaws. They cleared it wordlessly, stacking boxes on the floor. The sofa smelled of cat and old toast, springs poking through in places. They sat at opposite ends, knees almost touching, blankets pooled between them like a neutral zone. Morgan yawned. “If you snore, I’ll throw *Fifty Shades* at you.” “Fair.” He hesitated. “Thanks for—this. The roof. Not murdering me with a saucepan.” “Day’s not over.” She tucked her feet under herself, rugby shirt riding up. “Besides, you carried buckets. That counts.” Outside, a gust rattled the sign above the door. Inside, the only sound was the drip-drip-drip of water into metal and the soft wheeze of Gethin’s breathing evening out. Morgan watched the shadows shift across his face, the way exhaustion had sanded off the glossy edges. He looked younger, almost local. She thought: *He’ll be gone by morning.* The thought sat heavy, like wet wool. She shifted, blanket rustling. Gethin’s eyes opened a slit. “Still raining?” he mumbled. “Always raining.” She meant it as a joke, but it came out like a confession. He nodded, eyes closing again. His hand had migrated to the neutral zone between them, fingers relaxed, scar white against the blanket. Morgan stared at it a moment, then let her own hand rest nearby, not touching, just sharing the same square inch of warmth. The rain kept time above them, a lullaby neither had asked for.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 4 is a strong piece of intimate, low-key romantic writing that largely succeeds on its own terms. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. STRENGTHS: The dialogue is genuinely good. It has distinct voices: Morgan's dry, practical wit ("Day's not over") contrasts well with Gethin's performative self-deprecation ("I once carried an entire awards ceremony. Very heavy ego in that room."). The exchanges feel lived-in rather than constructed. The line about *Fifty Shades* being used as a table leg is both funny and character-revealing—it's exactly the kind of detail that earns its place. The banter about the saucepan tea and nettle soup is similarly grounded. The chapter follows its plan faithfully. The plan called for: storm worsening, water through skylight onto Morgan's duvet, hauling buckets, swearing, slipping, Gethin stripping the bed, Morgan yelling about clean sheets, UHT milk tea, talk turning to families, awkward silence that almost feels safe. Most of these beats land. The UHT milk becomes burnt-rubber saucepan tea (a reasonable substitution), the family talk is present though brief, and the ending achieves the "almost safe" quality the plan promised. Character consistency is high. Gethin's ego-deflection through anecdote ("I once did a film where the set flooded"), his grin deployed to avoid follow-up questions, his shoulders folding inward—all consistent with the profile. Morgan's violent efficiency with the sheets, her practical humor, her internal observation ("He'll be gone by morning") sitting heavy like wet wool—all ring true. The ending is genuinely well-handled. The two hands near each other in the neutral zone, not touching, is restrained and earned. "She meant it as a joke, but it came out like a confession" is one of the chapter's best lines—it does real emotional work without announcing itself. WEAKNESSES: Some metaphors strain. "Cold as melted coins" for the drip is slightly awkward—melted coins aren't particularly cold, and the image doesn't quite cohere. "Like a slow-motion stoning" for water dripping on the duvet is vivid but slightly overwrought for the register of the piece. "Doomed metronome" for the drip-ping is a bit self-conscious. These are minor but worth noting. The scar detail ("white, neat, like someone had tried to erase him") is the chapter's most forced moment. The simile is too literary, too pointed—it announces significance in a way that the rest of the chapter carefully avoids. It feels imported from a different, more melodramatic story. The family conversation is thinner than the plan suggested. "Cardiff feels like a play I walked out of at intermission. Everyone else knows the ending" is a decent line but it's doing a lot of heavy lifting for what should be a more textured exchange. The plan promised talk about families—hers still local, his scattered—but we get only Gethin's side, briefly. "Shadows jumped across stacks of books" and "shadows shift across his face" appear in close proximity, which is a minor repetition. The observation that he "looked younger, almost local" is slightly too on-the-nose given the themes. The chapter is not particularly surprising in its arc—two people thrown together by circumstance, sharing warmth, ending in near-touch—but it executes this familiar shape with enough specificity and restraint that it doesn't feel stale. Overall, this is confident, controlled writing that serves the story well. The flaws are real but minor. The emotional register is consistently maintained, the dialogue earns its keep, and the ending is genuinely moving without being sentimental. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The spaniel arrived at first light, announced by a single, businesslike bark and the squeak of the letterbox. Morgan unlatched the shop door to find him soaked, collarless, and wagging so hard his back paws danced. “Merlin, you daft sod, the bakery’s the other way.” Gethin, still wrapped in the rugby-shirt blanket, peered over her shoulder. “He looks like a used mop.” “He belongs to Jan next door. She leaves the door on the latch to air the kitchen and he hears strangers and panics.” The dog shook, sending a galaxy of raindrops across Gethin’s bare shins. He yelped. Merlin took this as an invitation and planted two muddy paws on Gethin’s jeans. Morgan tried to look stern and failed. “Sit.” Merlin sat, tail thumping. “Well trained,” Gethin said, crouching to scratch behind one floppy ear. “Only for people who sound like they mean it,” Morgan muttered, then caught herself smiling. They towelled the dog with faded tea-towels that emerged the colour of weak cocoa. Merlin, once dry, began a leisurely tour of the shop, sniffing Crime with judicial seriousness before flopping across the travel guides like he owned the place. “Coffee,” Morgan declared. “Dog hair in it is extra.” She flicked the kettle; it clicked on, then off again—the breaker had tripped overnight. She swore under her breath, reset it, and the little red light blinked back to life. While the kettle reheated, Gethin crouched to pick up a puzzle piece Merlin had nosed from its box—a corner of sky. He turned it between finger and thumb as if it might unlock something. “Jan tried to finish that one for six months,” Morgan said. “Gave up when she realised the sky and the sea were the same Pantone.” “Sounds like typecasting,” Gethin said. “Everything blue, nothing resolved.” They carried the coffee upstairs to the flat because Merlin had claimed the sofa. The mattress was still angled against the wall, drying in a draft that smelled of plaster and yesterday’s storm. Morgan set two chipped mugs on the windowsill and wrestled the sash up; cool salt air rushed in, chased by gull noise. Below them the harbour looked bruised—grey water, bobbing dinghies, holiday cottages shuttered against October. Gethin leaned out, elbows on the sill, hair tangling in the wind. “I tried to hire a boat here once. Got told the skipper wouldn’t take actors—said we talk to the horizon and forget to bring petrol.” Morgan laughed, a short surprised bark. “That’d be Dafydd Evans. He’s my uncle. He still tells that story at Christmas.” Silence settled, comfortable enough that Gethin could hear the small click of Morgan’s mug against her teeth. Merlin, left downstairs, gave a single mournful howl then settled. Morgan pulled out a shoebox from under the sill, lifted the lid. Inside were loose photos curled at the edges, postcards, a thick wodge of printed pages—her abandoned PhD. She hesitated, then handed Gethin a photograph: a younger Morgan in a university corridor, arms full of folders, fringe hacked short with kitchen scissors. “Four years in, two chapters to go,” she said. “Then Mam got sick. Emphysema. By the time I drove back the oxygen tanks had moved in.” Gethin studied the picture like it might bite. “I was filming in Budapest when my dad had his first heart scare. My agent wouldn’t let me fly back—said insurance wouldn’t cover reshoots. I sent flowers. He hates flowers.” He set the photo down gently. “He’s still alive, mostly. We talk birthdays and funerals.” Morgan nodded, eyes on the cardboard lid. “I talk to Mam through the till. Ring up a sale, tell her the gossip. Daft, but cheaper than therapy.” A gull landed on the sill, eyed the coffee, decided against. Morgan flicked the bird away with a tea-towel. “You ever think of walking off set? Just… not turning up?” “Every day. But the machine keeps moving. If you stop, it rolls over you and hires someone younger.” He tried a grin; it felt thin. “Plus I’ve forgotten how to fill a tax form.” She snorted. “I forget how not to.” Below, Merlin barked once, then again—urgent. Morgan peered down. Jan stood on the pavement in slippers, flour in her hair, calling the dog. Morgan whistled; Merlin shot out the door like a tawny bullet. Jan waved a relieved thanks; Morgan waved back. When she turned, Gethin was closer than she expected, coffee steam rising between them. For a moment the only sound was the gulls and the faint creak of the old sash. Morgan felt her pulse in
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter demonstrates considerable craft and largely delivers on the plan's promise of "accidents steering the plot" and dialogue that misfires productively. Let me examine it closely. STRENGTHS: The chapter opens with strong sensory specificity—the spaniel's "businesslike bark," the letterbox squeak, the tea-towels emerging "the colour of weak cocoa." These details feel earned rather than decorative. The dog functions exactly as planned: a comic interlude that creates physical proximity and emotional softening without forcing sentiment. The dialogue is genuinely good. "Only for people who sound like they mean it" is a sharp line that does double duty—it's about the dog but also about Gethin's performative charm. The exchange about Dafydd Evans the boat skipper is particularly well-executed: it's funny, it deepens Morgan's local roots, and it creates an unexpected connection that feels organic rather than contrived. "He still tells that story at Christmas" is exactly the kind of lived-in detail the plan promised. The backstory revelations—Morgan's abandoned PhD, Gethin's father's heart scare—are handled with admirable restraint. Neither character wallows. "I sent flowers. He hates flowers" is a beautifully compressed character moment. Morgan talking to her mother through the till is genuinely moving and consistent with her established voice. The chapter follows its plan faithfully: the dog arrives, they towel it, fingers almost brush (though this is handled more obliquely than planned—the "closer than she expected" moment substitutes for the ear-touching beat), the dog farts (present), the spell breaks but something softer remains. WEAKNESSES: The chapter ends mid-sentence: "Morgan felt her pulse in" — this is either a deliberate cliffhanger technique or an error. As a technique it's somewhat affected; as an error it's a significant flaw. Either way it feels incomplete and slightly gimmicky. The gull on the windowsill "eyed the coffee, decided against" is a small forced whimsy that doesn't quite land—it anthropomorphizes too cutely. The puzzle piece moment ("Everything blue, nothing resolved") is the chapter's most self-conscious metaphor. "Sounds like typecasting" is a bit too neat, too writerly—it's the kind of line that announces its own cleverness. The plan called for fingers brushing while towelling the dog's ear, both pulling back "as if burnt." The chapter softens this into a vaguer proximity moment upstairs. The physical near-miss is less crisp than planned, though the replacement scene has its own quiet tension. The chapter is slightly less funny than the plan suggested—the dog fart is present but brief, and the comedy of the dog interlude is somewhat subordinated to the emotional revelations upstairs. The plan's "spell breaks" beat is handled well, but the chapter's emotional weight tilts more toward the backstory exchange than the physical comedy. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: Both characters remain true to their profiles. Gethin's thin grin, his self-aware admission about the machine rolling over him, his careful handling of Morgan's photograph—all consistent. Morgan's "cheaper than therapy" line, her practical relationship with grief, her surprised bark of laughter—all ring true. OVERALL: This is accomplished literary fiction writing with genuine emotional intelligence. The truncated ending is the most significant flaw. The dialogue is largely excellent, the details are specific and earned, and the chapter advances the relationship with appropriate subtlety. It's not quite a masterpiece—some moments are slightly too polished, too aware of their own craft—but it's well above average work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 14 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 14 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The ferry left at six-fifteen sharp, and by five-thirty the sky over Aberaeron was the colour of old pewter. Gethin stood on the harbour wall with a duffel that looked as if it had been bought that morning—because it had. Inside were two T-shirts, a toothbrush, and the battered Welsh fairy-tale book now swollen with damp and thumbprints. He kept touching the spine, as though checking it was still there. Morgan had said she’d “walk down later, maybe,” which in Aberaeron meant either she would or she wouldn’t, and asking again would only make her dig her heels deeper. So he paced the slick cobbles, collar up, rehearsing lines that sounded worse each time. *Thanks for the roof. Thanks for the stories. Thanks for not murdering me with a saucepan.* None of them got near the thing he actually wanted to say. At five-forty-five she appeared, hands in the pockets of the same navy jumper, hair twisted up with what looked suspiciously like a train ticket. She carried nothing except a small, square package wrapped in yesterday’s *Western Mail*. When she reached him she didn’t stop, just jerked her chin toward the bench bolted to the wall. Salt crusted the slats; they sat anyway. “Boat’s on time,” she said, nodding at the white shape idling beyond the breakwater. “For once.” He rubbed his palms on his jeans. “Look, I—” She thrust the parcel at him. “Plane reading. Don’t open it here.” He turned it over. The newspaper was already soft from sea air. “What if it’s contraband?” “Then you’ll finally have a headline that isn’t about your ex.” A gull landed on the rail beside them, bold as a landlord. Morgan flapped a hand; it lifted off, screaming. Somewhere behind them a car door slammed, an engine coughed, but the town felt muffled, holding its breath. “I’ll bring the book back,” he said. “Accent fixed, like promised.” “No rush. I’ve others.” She stared at the ferry, not at him. “Anyway, you might decide Welsh consonants aren’t worth the jet-lag.” He laughed, a small sound swallowed by wind. “I once spent three hours learning to roll an R for a Russian oligarch role. I think I can manage a ll.” Her mouth twitched. “Prove it in public and they’ll think you’re having a stroke.” Silence again. The ferry hooted once, low and mournful. Gethin’s pulse beat stupidly fast. He told himself it was caffeine, not fear. “Listen,” he started, then stopped, because the next word refused to leave. Morgan saved him. “You know that sofa? Springs have gone. You left an arse-print. I flipped the cushion.” “Permanent damage, then.” “Like you were never there.” She said it lightly, but her fingers worried the cuff of her sleeve. He risked a look at her profile: the freckle on the bridge of her nose, the small scar through her left eyebrow where a book spine had caught her years ago. Details he’d stored without meaning to. “Come to London,” he said, the words out before he could weigh them. “One weekend. I’ll show you the flat that still smells of paint and bad decisions. You can heckle the tourists on the South Bank.” She exhaled through her teeth. “I’ve got invoices, and a dog that howls if I’m late with breakfast. I don’t do weekends away.” “Not even for—” He stopped again. The word *me* sounded obscene in his mouth. Morgan turned then, fully facing him. Her eyes were the same brown as the harbour water, unreadable until you were close enough to see the flecks of gold. “You’ll be filming in Budapest next month. Then Vancouver. Then somewhere else with better tax breaks. I’m here when the tide comes in and when it goes out. That’s not a criticism. It’s just arithmetic.” He felt the sentence land, precise as a paper cut. “So I should say thanks and sail off?” “Or don’t say thanks. Sail anyway. Up to you.” The ferry sounded twice, final call. Gethin stood. Morgan stayed seated, arms folded, looking at her boots. He took one step, two, then turned back. “Can I ask a favour?” He held out his phone. “Take a picture. Proof I was here and nobody cared.” She rolled her eyes but took the phone. “Look less tragic.” He tried; the shutter clicked. She glanced at the screen, snorted. “You blinked. Perfect.” He pocketed the phone. “Send it if you want. Or delete it.” “I’ll decide later.” She stood too, suddenly close. The wind whipped her hair across his cheek, a brief sting of salt. They hovered, two people unsure of the choreography. Gethin leaned in; Morgan’s breath hitched—then a gull swooped low, wings brushing her head. She jerked back; he stepped sideways; the moment scattered like dropped coins. “Bugger off, bird,” she muttered. When she looked at him again, her face was flushed. “Go on, then.” He shouldered the duffel, parcel clutched against his ribs. At the gangway he paused, raised a hand. Morgan didn’t wave back. She stood with the sea at her shoulders, small against the grey, until he couldn’t tell where jumper ended and sky began. On the ferry deck he unwrapped the newspaper parcel. Inside was a slim blue book—Dylan Thomas, *Quite Early One Morning*. No note. Just a pressed gorse flower between pages 112 and 113, brittle and bright as a dropped coin. He closed it carefully, held it to his chest like a secret. The boat pulled away. Aberaeron shrank to a smear of pastel houses. Morgan was still on the bench, a darker speck against the wall. Gethin lifted a hand again, knowing she wouldn’t see. He whispered the Welsh word for thank-you—*diolch*—and the wind snatched it before it reached the shore. Behind him, gulls wheeled and screamed, sounding for all the world like laughter.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is Chapter 6 of the story, covering the farewell scene at the harbour. Let me assess it carefully against the plan, the character profiles, and the writing prompt. **Adherence to Chapter Plan:** The plan called for: bus service resumes, Gethin's car idles outside, he offers money for damage, Morgan refuses, she hands him the Welsh fairy-tale book with no inscription, he tries to promise return, she deflects with Amazon comment, door closes, bell still broken, Morgan finds his damp handprint and doesn't wipe it. The actual chapter diverges significantly — it's set at a harbour with a ferry rather than outside the shop, Morgan gives him Dylan Thomas rather than him leaving with the fairy-tale book (he already has it), and the handprint/bell ending is replaced with a ferry departure. The Amazon line doesn't appear. The plan's ending image is abandoned for a more conventionally romantic harbour farewell. This is a notable deviation, though the spirit of the scene (reluctant farewell, unspoken feelings, small gift) is preserved. **Dialogue Quality:** The dialogue is generally strong and in character. "Like you were never there" is a good line. "It's just arithmetic" is excellent — precise, Morgan-voiced, and emotionally loaded without being melodramatic. The banter about the photograph ("You blinked. Perfect.") feels natural and earned. The ll/stroke joke lands. However, some exchanges feel slightly too polished — the "permanent damage" / "like you were never there" exchange is almost too neat, a little theatrical. The Amazon deflection from the plan (which would have been sharper and more in character) is missed. **Character Consistency:** Gethin's fragile ego and charm are present but somewhat muted here — this is a more vulnerable, less roguish Gethin than the profile suggests. The profile's note about him performing love rather than doing it is gestured at but not fully dramatized. Morgan is well-rendered: her deflections, her arithmetic speech, her refusal to wave back. The "World's Okayest Daughter" mug doesn't appear, but that's fine. Both characters feel consistent with their profiles, if slightly softened for the farewell. **Prose Quality:** The prose is generally accomplished. "The sky over Aberaeron was the colour of old pewter" is competent but not startling. "Her eyes were the same brown as the harbour water, unreadable until you were close enough to see the flecks of gold" — this is the weakest line in the piece, a cliché of romantic description that the rest of the writing is too good to need. The gull interrupting the almost-kiss is a nice touch (consistent with the plan's spirit of accidents steering the plot), though it risks feeling contrived. "Brittle and bright as a dropped coin" for the gorse flower is a slightly strained simile — coins aren't particularly bright or brittle, and the comparison doesn't quite earn its place. **The Pressed Flower Problem:** The plan explicitly states "avoid symbolism that announces itself; no pressed flowers unless someone actually forgot to bin a wilted bouquet." The chapter ends with a pressed gorse flower between pages 112 and 113. This is precisely the kind of announced symbolism the plan warned against. It's a significant self-contradiction and tips the ending toward sentimentality. **Emotional Engagement:** The scene is genuinely moving in places. "That's not a criticism. It's just arithmetic" is the chapter's best moment. The almost-kiss interrupted by the gull is handled with light touch. The final image of Gethin whispering *diolch* into the wind is affecting, though it risks preciousness. The gulls "sounding for all the world like laughter" as a closing line is a bit too neat — it reaches for resonance but lands on the expected. **Originality:** The harbour farewell is a well-worn romantic trope. The plan's version (outside the shop, handprint on counter, broken bell) would have been more distinctive and more consistent with the story's established setting and texture. The ferry departure is more conventionally cinematic and less idiosyncratic than what was planned. **Overall:** This is skilled, emotionally competent writing with some genuinely good moments, but it softens the edges of both characters and reaches for conventional romantic imagery (harbour, ferry, pressed flower, whispered word) where the plan called for something more grounded and specific. The deviation from the plan costs it, as does the pressed flower and the eye-colour cliché. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 9 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 6 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 13 Characters Consistent with Profile: 14 Followed Chapter Plan: 7 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 14

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
Gethin’s trailer smelled of fresh paint and the over-buttered popcorn the runner insisted on refilling. Somewhere beyond the thin walls, a crane groaned and a generator coughed diesel. He’d been back in London for four days, had shot two scenes, and still hadn’t looked up the second assistant’s name. The fairy-tale book lay on the tiny fold-out table, corners soft from airport humidity. He kept opening it and closing it without reading, as though the story might have rewritten itself in Aberaeron dialect while he flew. His phone buzzed: a message from Cate, his agent. *You’re trending again, darling. “Mystery woman in wool shop?” Really?* Attached was a blurry shot—Morgan squinting at a delivery van, cardigan sleeves shoved high. Someone had added an orange circle around her head like a discount sticker. Gethin’s stomach clenched. He typed back: *Leave it alone.* Three dots, then nothing. Cate hated being told to leave anything alone. On set, they were shooting a rooftop confrontation in Docklands. Between takes he hid behind a stack of fake HVAC units and listened to the director drone about “authentic stakes.” Authentic stakes, Gethin thought, were a leaking skylight and a spaniel that farted when nervous. He kept the blue Dylan Thomas book in his jacket pocket now, the gorse flower flattened further every time he sat. He worried it would crumble to yellow dust before he figured out what to do. At lunch he sat with the sound mixer, a woman named Priya who’d once spent a summer in Aberaeron. She mentioned the harbour lights, the smell of fried cockles. Gethin’s pulse jumped like a bad cue. He didn’t say he knew the place; instead he asked about tide times, pretending it was research for a “passion project.” Priya laughed. “Tides wait for no actor, love.” Evening wrapped at nine, the fake rooftop skyline lit orange like perpetual sunset. In the Uber back to Primose Hill he opened the notes app and recorded himself mumbling Welsh: *Diolch am y llyfr. Dw i’n trio.* The app auto-corrected “llyfr” to “lifter.” He deleted it, tried again. The driver glanced in the mirror. Gethin shut the phone and stared out at London sliding past—every window lit, none of them hers. The flat was half-furnished: one sofa, one lamp, a television he never switched on. He set the parcel—the newspaper still smelled faintly of sea—on the coffee table and stared at it like it might explode. When he finally tore the tape, the *Western Mail* headline underneath announced the local literary festival: *CELEBRITY GUESTS TBA*. His own face stared back from last year’s column, grinning like a game-show host. Below the fold: a black-and-white photo of Morgan beside a piece on “Independent Bookshops Fighting Back.” Her quote: *If Amazon’s the storm, we’re the leaky roof—still standing, if damp.* He laughed aloud, then felt oddly exposed, as if she’d known he’d read it. At 2:15 a.m. he sent the text he’d spent four days composing: *leak in my ceiling still dripping. tried to fix it with your accent. came out “lleeech”. send help, or at least tea bags. g.* He added the dragon emoji because the others felt too loud, then deleted it. Added a gorse-flower emoji instead—only to discover there wasn’t one. He sent it plain. The message hung in the bubble, delivered, then read. She didn’t reply. He told himself that was normal for people who had invoices and tides to mind. The next morning the tabloids had the story anyway. “Bookish Beauty Who Tamed Tinseltown Bad Boy?” printed beside a grainy zoom of the harbour bench. Someone had Photoshopped a heart-shaped lens flare between them. Cate rang, offering a “soft-focus exclusive”—a joint interview, charity angle, very good optics. Gethin declined so sharply Cate went quiet, which was worse than shouting. He spent the day shooting retakes; every time he said his character’s big romantic line, he heard his own voice crack on the word *stay*. They cut eventually, the director muttering about “needing real emotion.” Gethin wanted to laugh and say real emotion didn’t fit in a 35-millimetre frame. That evening he walked to the South Bank—no hood, no cap, just rain and the certainty that no one in London looked up from their phone long enough to recognise him. He bought a takeaway tea from a kiosk; it tasted of cardboard and wishful thinking. Beside the river he opened the blue book and read the poem Morgan had dog-eared: *I must go now, the tide’s at the turn.* The gorse flower fluttered out, landed on the pavement, was instantly speared by a passing trainer. He rescued the bruised petal, slipped it inside the book again, and felt the ridiculous sting of tears behind his eyes. He blamed the wind off the Thames. Back in the flat he drafted an email to the festival organiser, fingers stiff on the keys: *Would consider an appearance if Aberaeron’s own Morgan Evans introduces me. No fee required.* He hovered over send, heart hammering like the first time he’d stepped onstage. Then he hit delete, closed the laptop, and pressed the bruised petal between his fingers until it left a faint yellow smear. It smelled faintly of coconut oil and salt and something he couldn’t name but suddenly wanted to bottle. At 3:07 a.m. his phone buzzed once. A single line from Morgan: *Nettle tea’s better for leaks. And the accent’s improving. Slightly.* No emoji. No sign-off. Just the words, sitting on his screen like a flare. Gethin read them until the letters blurred, then typed back: *How slightly?* He didn’t expect an answer; when none came, he wasn’t disappointed. He fell asleep with the phone on his chest, the gorse petal stuck to his thumb like a promise he hadn’t yet decided to keep.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 7 is a transitional chapter—the "glitches" chapter as planned—and it largely succeeds at what transitional chapters must do: sustain emotional momentum without the crutch of shared physical space. The writing is confident and the voice is consistent. Let me examine it critically. STRENGTHS: The chapter follows its plan faithfully. The tabloid intrusion, Gethin's London isolation, the voice note (here rendered as a notes-app recording), Morgan's shop flooding with gawkers (implied through the tabloid coverage), and the late-night text exchange all appear. The plan's instruction to show Gethin rereading the book and Morgan's sign about non-buyers is handled obliquely but present in spirit. Character consistency is strong. Gethin's ego-fragility is rendered through small, precise details: he hasn't learned the second assistant's name, he performs research interest to Priya rather than admitting genuine feeling, he drafts and deletes obsessively. The detail of the notes app autocorrecting "llyfr" to "lifter" is genuinely funny and earns its place. Morgan's single text—"Nettle tea's better for leaks. And the accent's improving. Slightly."—is pitch-perfect: dry, warm, withholding, exactly her voice. The dialogue, though sparse (this is largely an interior chapter), is well-handled. Cate's "Mystery woman in wool shop?" and Priya's "Tides wait for no actor, love" are economical and characterful. The text exchange at the end is the chapter's emotional climax and it lands without sentimentality. The chapter avoids purple prose for the most part. "Every window lit, none of them hers" is the kind of line that could tip into self-parody but is restrained enough to work. The South Bank scene is the chapter's most atmospheric passage and mostly earns its imagery. WEAKNESSES AND CRITICISMS: The gorse flower continuity is slightly muddled. In Chapter 7 it's described as a "gorse flower flattened further every time he sat" in his jacket pocket, then later it "fluttered out" of the blue book on the South Bank. This is a minor internal inconsistency—is it in his pocket or in the book?—that a careful editor would catch. Some metaphors don't quite land. "The message hung in the bubble, delivered, then read" is functional but the word "hung" is slightly off—messages don't hang, they sit. "His pulse jumped like a bad cue" is a reasonable actor metaphor but feels slightly mechanical. "Heart hammering like the first time he'd stepped onstage" is the chapter's most clichéd simile—it's the kind of comparison that announces itself as literary without earning it. The Western Mail newspaper detail—where Gethin finds Morgan's quote about "leaky roofs"—is a little too convenient. The quote is too perfectly thematically resonant ("If Amazon's the storm, we're the leaky roof—still standing, if damp"), and it arrives at exactly the right moment to mirror Gethin's emotional state. This is the chapter's most "arranged" moment, where the author's hand is visible. The Priya scene, while charming, is slightly underdeveloped. She's introduced, does her thematic work (Aberaeron, tides), and disappears. This is fine for a minor character but the "passion project" lie feels like it should have more consequence or irony than it gets. The chapter is primarily interior/reflective, which is appropriate for the plan but means the "primarily in dialogue" instruction from the original prompt is necessarily set aside. This is justified by the chapter's structural position, but worth noting. The ending is well-judged. "A promise he hadn't yet decided to keep" is the right note of suspension—not too resolved, not too withholding. The gorse petal on his thumb is a good final image, though "something he couldn't name but suddenly wanted to bottle" edges toward the vague. Overall, this is accomplished, emotionally intelligent writing that serves the story's architecture well. It's not flawless—a few metaphors misfire, one plot convenience is too neat—but it demonstrates genuine craft and character understanding. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 15 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 14

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The bell still didn’t ring. Gethin pushed the door with his shoulder and stepped into The Crooked Shelf on a Thursday that smelled of early mackerel and late daffodils. No hoodie this time, no baseball cap, just a soft tweed jacket too thin for the wind and the same duffel he’d left with—now scuffed at the corners. The shop was quiet except for the soft click of a computer fan and the slow creak of old shelves settling their bones. Morgan was halfway up a ladder again, back to the door, pinning a hand-written sign above the poetry section: NEW ARRIVALS – HANDLE WITH SWEAR WORDS. She hadn’t heard him. He stood a moment, watching the efficient twist of her wrist, the way her sock had slid down into her boot. A small, ridiculous happiness rose in his throat. “Still not fixed the bell, I see,” he said. Morgan’s hand froze mid-staple. She turned, one eyebrow arched high enough to hook the sky. “Still not learned to knock?” “Door was open.” “Door’s always open. Doesn’t mean you’re welcome.” But she was already climbing down, rungs groaning under her weight. When her feet hit the rug she didn’t move closer; she simply folded her arms and looked at him, head tilted like a question mark. He held up the duffel. “Sofa volunteer reporting for duty.” Her gaze dropped to the bag, then rose to his face. “You left in a ferry.” “I came back on the coach. Cheaper. Slower. More time to practise rolling my r’s.” He attempted it: *Aberaer-on*. It still sounded like a sneeze. Morgan snorted. “Coach terminates at the top of the hill. That’s a mile and a half with luggage.” “I counted every step.” He set the bag down, nudged it with his toe. Inside were three paperbacks he’d bought second-hand in Cardiff, two T-shirts, and the blue Dylan Thomas with the gorse petal now flattened to a yellow ghost. He didn’t mention the petal; it felt too private for daylight. She glanced at the duffel again, as if expecting it to unzip itself and reveal hidden cameras. “How long?” “Depends how long the springs on your sofa last.” “My sofa’s currently hosting a colony of dust mites. They’ve unionised.” “I’ll negotiate.” Silence stretched—not empty, just the kind you can lean against without falling. Morgan scratched her wrist, leaving a faint white line. “You left a job.” “Left a schedule. Jobs can be rescheduled.” He tried a shrug; it felt clumsy, too loud in the quiet shop. She studied him a moment longer, then turned toward the back room. “Kettle’s cold. You can light the camping stove if you remember which knob hates you.” He followed her through the narrow doorway into the tiny kitchenette. The camping stove sat on the counter like a stubborn pet. He flicked the ignition; it hissed, caught, blue flame steady. Morgan leaned against the fridge, arms still folded, watching. “Jan’s dog still howling?” he asked, to fill the space. “Only when the postman’s late. Or early. Or exists.” She reached past him for mugs—same chipped set, one now missing its handle. Their fingers brushed; neither moved away. Water boiled. She poured. Steam clouded the small window. Outside, a fishing boat puttered past the breakwater, gulls wheeling behind it like thrown confetti. “Inventory’s up thirty percent since the photo,” she said suddenly. “People wanting selfies with the ‘mystery woman’. I put up a sign: *Books, not exhibits*. They still queue.” He winced. “I can issue a statement—tell them you hate me.” “They’d only think it’s romantic.” She handed him a mug. “Try the tea. It’s nettle. Allegedly fixes everything.” He sipped. It tasted like lawn clippings and hope. “Still leaks?” he asked, nodding toward the ceiling. “Only when it rains,” she deadpanned. “Which, in Wales, is a design flaw.” They carried the mugs back to the shop. Merlin had materialised from nowhere and was asleep on the poetry shelf, snoring gently. Morgan shooed him off; he relocated to the duffel and curled up with the air of a dog who understood luggage better than humans. Gethin sat on the edge of the sofa—springs twanged, but held. Morgan took the opposite end, leaving the middle cushion as neutral territory. She tucked one foot under herself, the other boot tapping a soft, nervous rhythm. “I googled tax forms,” he said. “Turns out they’re just very boring scripts.” She huffed a laugh. “And?” “I filled one in. Got halfway before I remembered I don’t actually owe anything to Wales yet.” “That’s the spirit—start small.” Silence again, softer now. The kind that invited confession but didn’t demand it. Morgan spoke first, voice low. “I kept thinking you’d send a postcard. Or a publicist.” “I sent a text. You answered.” He pulled the phone from his pocket, opened the thread: *Nettle tea’s better for leaks.* Below it, his unanswered *How slightly?* still sat like a stranded boat. “Figured the next move had to be analog.” She studied the screen, then him. “Analog’s messier.” “I’m learning to like mess.” Merlin farted in his sleep, a small, dignified sound. They both laughed, startled, and the tension snapped like an overstretched elastic band. Morgan leaned forward, elbows on knees. “Look, I’m not short of volunteers. The chip shop wants shelf space. The WI wants readings. What I’m short of is… continuity. Someone who knows where the Sellotape lives.” “I can learn Sellotape.” He hesitated. “I can learn a lot of things. Slowly. Badly.” She considered. “The sofa’s free until the end of the month. After that, rent’s payable in book recommendations and uncomplaining trips to the cash-and-carry.” “Terms accepted.” “And you fix the bell. I’m tired of surprises.” He glanced at the door. “I might need instructions.” “Instructions are in the drawer marked *miscellaneous*. Along with the fuse wire and my mother’s old raffle tickets.” She stood. “Come on, then.” They walked to the door. Morgan pulled open the drawer—rusty scissors, half a candle, and a small brass bell with a frayed ribbon. She handed it to him. “You drill, I’ll hold the ladder.” Outside, the wind had dropped; gulls circled in slow, indifferent loops. Gethin balanced on the step stool, screwing the bell back into the lintel. Morgan steadied the stool, one hand on his calf, warm through denim. The bell clanged once, clear and bright, then settled into a softer chime. He stepped down. They stood on the threshold, neither moving in or out. Somewhere below, the tide sighed against the harbour wall. “So,” Morgan said, “next customer rings that, you’ll have to shelve them yourself.” “I’ll try not to look too pleased about it.” She gave him the ghost of a smile, the chipped incisor flashing. “You’ll manage.” Merlin barked from inside, a single, authoritative note. They both turned, then looked back at each other. Gethin held out his hand—palm up, an offering, not a demand. Morgan hesitated, then laid her fingers across his. Her skin was cool, ink-stained, utterly real. The bell above them gave a soft, accidental ring as the wind nudged the door. They didn’t kiss; they didn’t need to. The touch was enough—an unspoken contract, renewable daily, payable in small kindnesses and bad jokes. Inside, the kettle began to boil again, the camping stove clicking like an old clock. Gethin squeezed her hand once, let go, and stepped over the threshold. Merlin thumped his tail against the rug in welcome. Morgan followed, pulling the door closed behind them. The bell chimed—once, bright and definite—then settled into silence, waiting for the next story to walk in.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 8 is the concluding chapter of this short story, and it largely succeeds at what it sets out to do: deliver a quiet, earned resolution that resists melodrama. Let me assess it carefully across the relevant dimensions. STRENGTHS: The dialogue is genuinely strong throughout. Lines like "Door's always open. Doesn't mean you're welcome" and "Only when it rains. Which, in Wales, is a design flaw" feel lived-in and character-specific.
